proposals.title: Quantifying the Modulation of 16-Day Planetary Wave Amplitude by the Quasi-Biennial Oscillation in the Stratosphere and Lower Mesosphere
proposals.motivation: The 16-day planetary wave is a key mode of atmospheric variability influencing global circulation and stratosphere-troposphere coupling. The Quasi-Biennial Oscillation (QBO) is known to modulate the extratropical stratospheric waveguide, affecting wave propagation. However, a systematic, quantitative assessment of the QBO's impact on 16-day wave amplitude across seasons and altitudes, particularly using modern reanalysis datasets, remains incomplete, limiting our understanding of this dynamical interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: The amplitude of the 16-day planetary wave exhibits a statistically significant, seasonally dependent modulation by the QBO phase, with stronger amplification during the QBO easterly phase in winter, and a distinct, potentially weaker, response in summer.
proposals.method: We will analyze ERA5 reanalysis data (pre-2026) from 1979 onward. The 16-day wave amplitude will be extracted via spectral analysis (e.g., wavelet transform) and band-pass filtering (12-20 day period) of geopotential height fields. The QBO phase will be defined using zonal wind at 50 hPa. The association will be quantified through composite analysis (QBO-E vs. QBO-W) and linear regression models, stratified by season (DJF, JJA) and altitude (stratosphere to lower mesosphere). Statistical significance will be assessed using Student's t-tests and bootstrap methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear, quantitative climatology of the QBO-16-day wave link, filling a gap in the understanding of middle atmospheric wave-mean flow interactions. The results will improve the mechanistic interpretation of seasonal forecasting models and contribute to the broader effort of understanding stratospheric influences on surface weather.
proposals.novelty: While the QBO's influence on other waves (e.g., the 10-day wave) has been studied, a focused, seasonal, and altitude-resolved quantification specifically for the 16-day wave using the full ERA5 record represents a targeted advance beyond earlier, more limited studies.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Forecasting 16-Day Wave Amplitude from Ground-Based Radar Deformation Time Series and Atmospheric Indices
proposals.motivation: The accurate forecasting of atmospheric wave activity, particularly the 16-day wave, is crucial for understanding middle atmosphere dynamics and improving numerical weather prediction models. Pre-2026 research has established links between wave amplitude, background wind conditions, and the Quasi-Biennial Oscillation (QBO), but predictive models based on these relationships remain largely linear and limited. Leveraging long-term, high-resolution ground-based radar datasets, which are routinely used for geohazard monitoring, offers a novel data source to build more robust, data-driven forecasting tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on long-term ground-based radar-derived atmospheric deformation time series, combined with background wind and QBO indices, can significantly outperform traditional statistical methods in predicting the 16-day wave amplitude with a 16-day lead time.
proposals.method: We will process long-term (10+ year) deformation time series from existing ground-based radar networks (e.g., GB-SAR used in landslide studies) to extract signals related to atmospheric waves. These time-series features will be combined with reanalysis data for background zonal wind and QBO phase indices. We will implement and compare several pre-2026 ML architectures, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Gradient Boosting Regressors (e.g., XGBoost), to model the non-linear relationships. The models will be trained to predict wave amplitude 16 days ahead, using a rolling-window validation scheme to assess performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first operational ML model for 16-day wave forecasting using ground-based radar data, directly contributing to atmospheric science. It will demonstrate the secondary utility of geotechnical monitoring infrastructure for climatological studies. The resulting framework will provide a template for integrating geodetic time series with climate indices for improved medium-range atmospheric predictions.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of ground-based radar deformation data, typically used for slope stability analysis, as a primary input for atmospheric wave forecasting is unprecedented. Furthermore, the integration of this geodetic data stream with climate indices within a non-linear ML framework to address a specific atmospheric prediction problem represents a significant interdisciplinary advance beyond existing linear correlation studies.

proposals.title: Global 16-Day Atmospheric Wave Mapping via a Multi-Source SAR Network: 3D Structure and Propagation from Ground-Based and Spaceborne Radar
proposals.motivation: The 16-day atmospheric wave, a fundamental planetary-scale oscillation, significantly influences global atmospheric dynamics and weather patterns. Its three-dimensional structure and propagation mechanisms remain incompletely characterized due to the sparse and disparate nature of observational networks. A unified analysis using high-resolution, continuous radar data can bridge critical gaps in understanding its forcing and dissipation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a globally distributed network of ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and spaceborne InSAR platforms can be synergistically combined to resolve the full three-dimensional structure and global propagation characteristics of the 16-day wave, revealing previously unresolved regional modulation and vertical coupling.
proposals.method: We will establish a processing framework to integrate deformation time series from a global selection of operational and archival GB-SAR systems (e.g., IBIS, GPRI) with multi-satellite InSAR stacks (Sentinel-1, ALOS-2). The method will apply advanced time-series InSAR algorithms (e.g., SBAS, PSI) to each data source, followed by spatiotemporal filtering to isolate the 16-day signal. A 3D tomographic inversion scheme, constrained by the different viewing geometries, will be used to reconstruct vertical profiles. Propagation vectors will be derived via cross-correlation and wavenumber-frequency analysis across the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global, three-dimensional empirical model of the 16-day wave derived from direct radar measurements. It will quantify the wave's interaction with major topographic features and its vertical energy transport, providing a new benchmark for validating general circulation models (GCMs) and improving medium-range weather forecasting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the first dedicated use of a heterogeneous global SAR network—combining the high temporal resolution of ground-based systems with the broad spatial coverage of satellites—for atmospheric wave tomography, moving beyond single-site studies or model reanalyses.

proposals.title: A Multi-Sensor Framework for Enhanced Landslide Precursor Detection: Integrating High-Resolution Optical Data with Ground-Based and Spaceborne InSAR
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, landslide hazard assessment primarily relies on InSAR and ground-based radar, which excel at measuring deformation but have limitations in spatial resolution and sensitivity to small-scale, pre-failure surface changes. These subtle changes, such as vegetation stress, minor cracking, or soil moisture variations, often precede major slope movement but are not reliably captured by SAR alone. Integrating higher-resolution optical data can bridge this gap, enabling earlier detection of incipient slope instability, particularly for smaller or more complex landslides.
proposals.hypothesis: The synergistic integration of high-resolution optical data (Landsat-8/9, Sentinel-2) with ground-based and spaceborne InSAR measurements will significantly improve the detection sensitivity and spatial characterization of early-stage landslide deformation and its environmental precursors compared to using SAR data alone.
proposals.method: The method will develop a multi-tiered monitoring framework. First, time-series InSAR (e.g., SBAS or PSI) from Sentinel-1 and ground-based radar will provide the core deformation chronology. Concurrently, Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-2 data will be processed to derive high-resolution (10-30m) indices for vegetation health (NDVI), soil moisture (NDMI), and surface temperature (LST). A data fusion algorithm, such as a multivariate statistical model or a machine learning regressor (e.g., Random Forest), will be trained to correlate temporal changes in these optical indices with InSAR-derived deformation rates. The framework will be validated on known landslide sites with diverse deformation histories.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operationalizable data fusion protocol for landslide early warning systems. It will advance the field by quantitatively linking pre-failure surface environmental changes with millimeter-scale deformation, moving beyond post-event analysis. The resulting framework will provide a more holistic understanding of landslide initiation processes and improve risk zonation maps.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor approaches exist, the systematic, algorithm-driven fusion of high-resolution optical indices specifically for detecting sub-centimeter deformation precursors in slopes is non-trivial and not standardized. The novelty lies in the targeted integration of freely available Sentinel-2 data (10m resolution) to resolve small-scale environmental changes directly relevant to slope mechanics, a refinement over previous coarser optical integrations.

proposals.title: A Multi-Sensor Fusion Framework for Landslide Deformation Monitoring: Integrating Ground-Based Radar, Spaceborne InSAR, and Optical Data via a Factor Graph Approach
proposals.motivation: Current landslide monitoring often relies on single-sensor systems (e.g., InSAR, GB-SAR) or simple multi-sensor comparisons, which fail to provide a unified, high-frequency, and geometrically consistent deformation state. This limits early warning capabilities and the understanding of complex slope kinematics. A robust data fusion methodology is needed to leverage the complementary strengths of optical (high spatial detail, geometric accuracy) and radar (high precision deformation, all-weather) sensors for comprehensive slope stability assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a probabilistic sensor fusion framework, adapted from robotics, can generate a more accurate, continuous, and interpretable 4D deformation field of a landslide than any single sensor or heuristic combination of data, by explicitly modeling the spatio-temporal correlations and uncertainties of each data source.
proposals.method: We will adapt a factor graph-based fusion architecture, specifically a pose graph optimization, to integrate time-series from GB-SAR (high temporal resolution), Sentinel-1/ALOS-2 InSAR (wide-area coverage), and high-resolution optical imagery (e.g., PlanetScope, UAV) for geometric correction and feature tracking. Deformation measurements from each sensor will be modeled as probabilistic constraints (factors) connecting nodes representing the landslide's state (3D displacement) over time. Optimization (using libraries like gtsam) will produce the most likely deformation trajectory, filling temporal gaps and reducing atmospheric and geometric errors inherent in individual techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source processing framework for landslide monitoring that provides a unified, uncertainty-quantified 4D deformation product. It will advance the field from parallel multi-sensor analysis to true synergistic fusion, potentially improving deformation detection thresholds and temporal resolution. The methodology will be validated on well-instrumented sites like the Woda landslide, setting a new standard for integrative geohazard monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor studies exist, the application of a formal probabilistic fusion framework (factor graphs) from robotics to landslide geodesy is novel. This moves beyond temporal or spatial interpolation to a rigorous estimation-theoretic approach that directly models the underlying physical state and all observational uncertainties.

proposals.title: Resolving Mesoscale Atmospheric Wavefronts for Slope Stability Assessment: A Novel Application of Seismic Array Processing to Sparse MLT Wind Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Yi Luo and others has established that atmospheric loading, including pressure waves and winds, can influence landslide deformation and slope stability. However, characterizing the spatiotemporal structure of these atmospheric forcings, particularly in the Mesosphere-Lower-Thermosphere (MLT) region, is challenging with sparse observational networks. Improved resolution of these wavefields is critical for understanding their coupling to surface geomechanics and integrating them into slope stability early warning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that seismic array processing techniques, specifically frequency-wavenumber (f-k) analysis and beamforming, can be successfully adapted to resolve the wavenumber spectra and propagation directions of atmospheric waves from spatially sparse MLT wind measurement arrays, providing a novel constraint for atmospheric forcing models used in slope stability analysis.
proposals.method: We will adapt established seismic array methods, specifically frequency-wavenumber (f-k) analysis and delay-and-sum beamforming, to process time-series wind data from existing sparse MLT radar networks (e.g., meteor radars). The core technical approach involves treating the radar sites as a seismic array, computing cross-correlations between station pairs to construct a spatial covariance matrix, and applying high-resolution f-k spectral estimation (e.g., Capon's method) to resolve propagating wave modes. The derived wave parameters (phase velocity, direction) will be used to initialize and validate simplified atmospheric loading models, which will be correlated with InSAR-derived slope deformation time series from case study areas.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new methodological pipeline for extracting wavefield information from sparse atmospheric data, directly benefiting atmospheric dynamics research. For geohazards, it will provide a more physically constrained link between upper atmospheric dynamics and surface slope deformation, potentially improving the explanatory power of statistical models that link meteorological triggers to landslide activity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary application of well-established seismic signal processing techniques to a fundamentally different physical domain (atmospheric winds) to address a geotechnical problem (slope stability). While f-k analysis is standard in seismology, its application to sparse MLT wind arrays for the purpose of geohazard assessment is unprecedented in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Robust Multi-Sensor InSAR-GB-SAR Fusion Framework for Landslide Deformation Monitoring Under High Atmospheric and Cloudy Conditions
proposals.motivation: Current InSAR and ground-based radar (GB-SAR) deformation monitoring methods are highly effective under clear-sky, low atmospheric delay conditions. However, in mountainous landslide-prone regions, persistent cloud cover and high atmospheric water vapor variability (particularly at high airmass) introduce significant phase errors, degrading measurement precision and temporal continuity. This limits the reliability of early warning systems, as critical deformation signals can be masked or misinterpreted during adverse atmospheric periods.
proposals.hypothesis: By synergistically fusing multi-temporal InSAR (e.g., Sentinel-1) with continuous GB-SAR data and integrating high-resolution atmospheric models, it is possible to model and mitigate tropospheric delays under non-ideal conditions, thereby retrieving reliable deformation time series with quantified uncertainty for slopes in cloudy, high-humidity environments.
proposals.method: The approach will develop a two-stage correction and fusion pipeline. First, a pixel-based atmospheric phase screen (APS) estimation will be performed using iterative time-series InSAR (e.g., SBAS) combined with zenith total delay data from numerical weather models (e.g., ERA5) and GNSS stations. Second, this APS will be used to constrain the GB-SAR phase unwrapping and deformation retrieval during cloudy intervals via a Kalman filter framework that fuses the long-wavelength stability of corrected InSAR with the high temporal resolution of GB-SAR. Precision will be analyzed using a Monte Carlo simulation propagating errors from APS residuals, orbital inaccuracies, and geometric decorrelation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, operational methodology for retrieving millimeter-level slope deformation during periods traditionally considered unsuitable for radar monitoring. It will advance the field by providing a validated framework for 24/7, all-weather landslide monitoring, directly enhancing the reliability of geohazard early warning systems. The precision analysis will establish clear uncertainty bounds for decisions based on data acquired under high atmospheric noise.
proposals.novelty: While atmospheric correction in InSAR is well-studied, the targeted fusion with GB-SAR to create a resilient monitoring system specifically for the challenging, yet common, scenario of persistent cloud cover and high airmass in landslide areas is novel. The method moves beyond post-hoc correction to active retrieval optimization during data acquisition.

proposals.title: Assessing the Global Nature of Interhemispheric Landslide Deformation Correlations: A Multi-Site Ground-Based Radar Analysis in High-Latitude Regions
proposals.motivation: Recent studies using ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and InSAR have revealed potential interhemispheric correlations in landslide deformation patterns, suggesting a possible global climatic or seasonal driver. Confirming this phenomenon is crucial for advancing landslide early warning systems, as it could shift models from purely local to globally-informed predictive frameworks. However, these initial findings are based on limited site comparisons and require validation across diverse high-latitude environments to assess their universality.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed interhemispheric correlation in seasonal landslide deformation rates is a globally consistent phenomenon, detectable across multiple, geographically dispersed high-latitude sites in both the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Deformation time series from these sites will show statistically significant, anti-phase seasonal patterns linked to hemispheric climatic cycles.
proposals.method: We will deploy and analyze data from GB-SAR systems (e.g., IBIS-L, GPRI-II) at three new high-latitude sites per hemisphere, selected for active, slow-moving landslides. Time-series deformation will be extracted using Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) and Small Baseline Subset (SBAS) techniques tailored for GB-SAR. Correlation analysis will employ cross-correlation and wavelet coherence methods on the deformation time series, explicitly comparing phase lags and strength of correlation against local meteorological data (precipitation, temperature) and hemispheric climate indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-site, hemispheric-scale empirical test of a potentially global landslide deformation signal, moving beyond single-case-study correlations. It will establish a foundational dataset for validating or refining models that link large-scale climate forcing to local slope stability. The results will directly inform the spatial scalability of early warning systems that incorporate seasonal forecasts.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Luo and others combined GB-SAR and InSAR for single-slope or regional monitoring, this proposal systematically tests a specific global geophysical hypothesis using a coordinated multi-hemisphere GB-SAR network. The explicit focus on interhemispheric correlation across multiple independent sites, rather than just method combination, is a novel and necessary step to elevate the finding from an observation to a principle.

proposals.title: A Data Assimilation Framework for Enhanced State Estimation of Landslide Dynamics by Fusing Ground-Based Radar and Spaceborne InSAR
proposals.motivation: Current landslide monitoring often relies on separate analyses of high-frequency ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and wide-area spaceborne InSAR data, lacking a unified framework for dynamic state estimation. This gap limits the ability to produce a complete, physically consistent spatiotemporal picture of slope deformation, which is critical for accurate early warning and understanding failure mechanisms. Integrating these complementary data streams is a key challenge in pre-2026 geohazard research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential data assimilation (DA) framework can optimally fuse GB-SAR and InSAR observations with a simplified mechanical model to produce a more accurate, complete, and dynamically consistent estimate of landslide displacement and velocity fields than either dataset or a simple combination can provide.
proposals.method: We will develop a sequential Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) framework. The state vector will include displacement and velocity at model grid points. A visco-plastic or empirical creep model will serve as the forward model to propagate the state. GB-SAR line-of-sight (LOS) time series and multi-temporal InSAR-derived LOS displacement maps will be assimilated as observations, with careful handling of their different temporal resolutions, spatial coverages, and error covariances. The system will be tested on a well-instrumented case study, such as the Woda landslide.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel operational DA pipeline for landslide monitoring that provides continuous, gap-free state estimates and uncertainty quantification. It will advance the field from comparative multi-sensor analysis to integrated dynamical analysis, offering a foundational tool for improving the temporal resolution and physical consistency of deformation products used in early warning systems.
proposals.novelty: While DA is established in meteorology and oceanography, its application to fuse GB-SAR and InSAR for landslide state estimation is novel. The proposed framework moves beyond post-hoc data comparison to real-time, model-constrained synthesis, addressing the spatiotemporal scale mismatch between these key radar datasets in a principled way.

proposals.title: Decadal-Scale Deformation Climatology of Unstable Slopes: A Multi-Sensor InSAR and Ground-Based Radar Analysis for Improved Landslide Hazard Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current landslide deformation analyses often rely on InSAR time series of five years or less, which is insufficient to capture the full spectrum of climatic forcing and its interannual variability. A longer-term deformation record is critical to distinguish transient seasonal movements from persistent, climate-driven accelerations that signal increased slope instability. This gap limits the ability to develop robust early-warning thresholds and forecast models under changing precipitation and temperature regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending the deformation time series to a decade or more will reveal distinct, climatically modulated deformation regimes and multi-year trends that are not discernible in shorter datasets. These long-term patterns can be quantitatively linked to antecedent precipitation indices and temperature anomalies, providing a predictive framework for slope stability.
proposals.method: We will compile and process a multi-sensor SAR archive (e.g., Sentinel-1, ALOS-2, and archived ENVISAT/ERS data) to generate a coherent deformation time series exceeding ten years for selected high-risk slopes in China. The Small BAseline Subset (SBAS) InSAR technique will be used for long-term trend analysis, while Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) will be applied in urbanized areas. This spaceborne data will be temporally fused and validated with available long-term ground-based radar (GB-SAR) campaigns. Statistical time-series analysis (e.g., Mann-Kendall trend tests, wavelet analysis) will be employed to characterize interannual variability and correlate deformation phases with long-term meteorological reanalysis data (e.g., ERA5).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first 'deformation climatology' for specific landslide-prone regions, establishing a baseline of long-term slope behavior. It will advance the field by providing a methodology to integrate decadal-scale multi-sensor SAR data for slope stability evaluation. The results will directly inform regional landslide hazard models by quantifying the lag and cumulative effects of climate variables on slope deformation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor fusion is established, the systematic creation of a decadal-scale, climate-focused deformation record for slope stability represents a significant temporal extension beyond standard InSAR monitoring periods. The explicit focus on extracting and modeling interannual signals, rather than just seasonal or linear trends, moves beyond current state-of-the-art analyses.

proposals.title: Revealing the Subsurface Precursors of Landslide Deformation: A Multi-Sensor Framework Integrating Ground-Based Radar, InSAR, and Borehole Instrumentation
proposals.motivation: Current deformation monitoring for landslides, using ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and spaceborne InSAR, is primarily sensitive to surface movement. This creates a critical observational gap regarding the vertical structure and upward propagation of deep-seated deformation before it reaches the surface, limiting early warning capabilities. Understanding this full propagation path is essential for assessing the internal mechanics and failure evolution of complex, deep-seated landslides.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-temporal-resolution GB-SAR surface data with lower-altitude, subsurface displacement measurements from borehole inclinometers and distributed fiber optic sensing (DFOS) will enable the reconstruction of a complete 3D deformation field, revealing distinct precursor phases of deep-seated slope movement.
proposals.method: We will deploy a GB-SAR system (e.g., IBIS-L) for continuous, mm-precision surface monitoring. At strategic locations within the radar's footprint, we will install vertical boreholes instrumented with in-place inclinometers and DFOS cables to capture displacement profiles at depth. Time-series data from all sensors will be synchronized and fused using a Kalman filter framework. A novel data assimilation approach will be employed to inversely model the propagation of the deformation front from depth to the surface, correlating it with triggering factors like pore-water pressure from co-located piezometers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically derived models of deep-to-shallow deformation propagation for specific landslide types, moving beyond surface observation. It will establish a validated methodological framework for multi-sensor subsurface deformation tracing, directly contributing to improved physical models of landslide initiation and enhanced early warning systems that utilize deep-seated precursors.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor monitoring is established, the specific integration of GB-SAR with borehole-based sensing to actively trace and model the upward propagation of a deformation front, rather than just comparing independent datasets, is a novel approach to closing the observational gap between deep-seated processes and surface expression.

proposals.title: Imaging Atmospheric Disturbance Fields for Landslide Precursor Analysis Using Seismic Ambient Noise Cross-Correlation Tomography on Ground-Based Radar Data
proposals.motivation: Atmospheric phase screens (APS) are a major noise source in ground-based radar (GBR) and InSAR deformation time series, limiting the detection of subtle pre-failure slope movements. Current APS correction methods often rely on empirical filtering, which can inadvertently remove real deformation signals. A method to characterize the spatial structure and propagation of atmospheric waves directly from the radar data itself would improve signal separation and could reveal new geophysical precursors.
proposals.hypothesis: Cross-correlation tomography, adapted from seismic ambient noise analysis, can be applied to the spatio-temporal phase data from a multi-station GBR network to image the horizontal structure and advection direction of atmospheric water vapor waves, enabling their physical separation from true ground deformation.
proposals.method: We will process continuous phase histories from multiple Ground-Based Synthetic Aperture Radar (GB-SAR) units monitoring a single large slope. Treating each radar pixel as a 'station', we will compute cross-correlation functions of the phase time series between all pixel pairs to estimate empirical Green's functions of atmospheric wave propagation. Using a surface wave tomography framework (e.g., Eikonal or Helmholtz tomography), we will invert these correlations to produce 2D maps of phase velocity and directionality of the dominant atmospheric waves as a function of time. This will be integrated with concurrent meteorological data (pressure, humidity, wind) for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-based method for APS characterization and correction in radar deformation monitoring, moving beyond statistical filtering. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for understanding how local micro-meteorology interacts with complex terrain, potentially identifying atmospheric patterns that correlate with slope instability events.
proposals.novelty: While cross-correlation tomography is well-established in seismology, its application to image atmospheric structures using ground-based radar phase data is novel. This approach directly targets the spatial wavefield causing APS, unlike traditional temporal or spatial filtering methods used in InSAR/GBR processing pre-2026.

proposals.title: Assessing the Sufficiency of Single-Instrument GB-SAR Climatology for Characterizing Spatially Variable Slope Oscillations: A Multi-Sensor Validation Study
proposals.motivation: Long-term ground-based SAR (GB-SAR) time series are increasingly used to establish deformation climatologies for slopes, capturing seasonal and multi-year oscillation patterns. However, relying on a single GB-SAR instrument's point-of-view may inadequately characterize spatially heterogeneous deformation, as the radar's line-of-sight (LOS) geometry and limited spatial coverage can miss key 3D displacement components and off-axis movements. This limitation risks misrepresenting the true kinematic behavior and stability of complex landslides, a critical gap for pre-2026 slope disaster early warning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deformation climatology derived solely from a single, long-term GB-SAR dataset is insufficient to fully characterize the magnitude, direction, and spatial pattern of seasonal slope oscillations, leading to significant errors in kinematic interpretation when not validated with spatially complementary data.
proposals.method: We will select a well-instrumented, actively deforming slope (e.g., the Woda landslide) with a multi-year GB-SAR campaign. The GB-SAR time-series climatology will be processed using Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) and Small Baseline Subset (SBAS) algorithms. This single-instrument climatology will be rigorously compared against a 3D deformation field synthesized from pre-2026 multi-geometry spaceborne InSAR (Sentinel-1 ascending/descending), on-site GNSS, and meteorological data. Statistical metrics (e.g., cross-correlation, RMSE of velocity fields) and kinematic modeling will quantify the discrepancies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to evaluate the representativeness and limitations of single-perspective GB-SAR climatologies. It will deliver best-practice guidelines for integrating GB-SAR with other geospatial data to construct robust, multi-dimensional slope deformation models, directly enhancing the reliability of long-term stability assessments and early warning protocols.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor fusion is common, this study uniquely challenges the sufficiency of a *long-term climatology* from a single, trusted instrument—a foundational assumption in many monitoring programs—by formally testing its spatial completeness against a synthesized 3D ground truth.

proposals.title: Modeling the Coupling of Geomagnetic Activity and Landslide Deformation: A Multi-Sensor Data Fusion Approach
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that landslide deformation can exhibit periodic signals, including a ~6-hour oscillation, potentially linked to tidal forces. Concurrently, studies in space weather suggest geomagnetic activity can induce telluric currents and ground-level electromagnetic fields. The observed amplification of the 6h oscillation with geomagnetic activity points to a poorly understood geophysical coupling mechanism that could be a precursor signal for accelerated slope movement, offering a new avenue for early warning.
proposals.hypothesis: Enhanced geomagnetic activity during solar storms modulates near-surface pore pressure and/or induces micro-seismicity through electromagnetic forcing, which in turn amplifies the 6-hour tidal oscillation signal in landslide deformation measurements.
proposals.method: We will fuse long-term deformation time series from ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and spaceborne InSAR (e.g., Sentinel-1) for a selected active landslide (e.g., Woda) with concurrent geomagnetic indices (Kp, Dst) and local magnetometer data. A multivariate time-series analysis will be performed using Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD) or Singular Spectrum Analysis (SSA) to isolate the 6h oscillation component. We will then develop a statistical coupling model (e.g., a Vector Autoregression - VAR - model or a transfer function model) to quantify the relationship between the amplitude of the extracted 6h deformation signal and geomagnetic activity levels, controlling for meteorological variables (rainfall, temperature).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model linking specific geomagnetic activity parameters to landslide deformation dynamics. It will advance the understanding of exogenous triggers beyond rainfall and seismicity, potentially identifying a novel environmental precursor for slope instability. The methodology will demonstrate a novel fusion of space weather data with high-resolution geodetic monitoring for geohazard assessment.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Yi Luo and others has fused InSAR, GB-SAR, and meteorological data, the explicit integration and modeling of geomagnetic activity as a potential modulating factor for short-period landslide oscillations is a novel and unexplored direction in slope stability research.

proposals.title: Assimilating Meteorological Data to Model and Fill Temporal Gaps in Ground-Based and Spaceborne Radar Deformation Time Series for Landslide Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Continuous deformation time series from InSAR and ground-based radar are critical for early warning of landslides, but they are frequently interrupted by temporal decorrelation, geometric distortions, and atmospheric delays, creating hazardous data gaps. Current gap-filling methods often rely on statistical interpolation, which fails to capture the physical drivers of slope movement linked to hydro-meteorological forcing. Integrating the mature data assimilation frameworks from numerical weather prediction into geohazard monitoring presents a timely opportunity to create physically consistent, continuous displacement records.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a data assimilation framework, which integrates sparse radar displacement measurements with a physically-based slope stability model forced by high-resolution meteorological data, can accurately reconstruct continuous landslide deformation dynamics and reliably fill observation gaps.
proposals.method: We will employ an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) to assimilate displacement measurements from GB-SAR and Sentinel-1 InSAR into a coupled hydrological-mechanical model (e.g., a simplified TRIGRS model). The model will be driven by assimilated atmospheric data (precipitation, temperature, soil moisture) from the ERA5 reanalysis and local weather stations. The EnKF will update the model state (pore pressure, factor of safety) at each observation step, and during radar data gaps, the forward-running, meteorologically forced model will generate the best estimate of deformation, creating a seamless, physically-grounded time series.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-informed methodology for generating continuous landslide displacement histories, directly enhancing early warning system reliability. It will provide a formal framework for fusing multi-source radar data with meteorological forecasts, moving beyond purely statistical gap-filling towards a predictive, process-based monitoring paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is standard in meteorology, its application to fuse atmospheric data and radar observations for physically modeling and filling deformation gaps in landslide monitoring is novel. This approach moves beyond simple interpolation or independent correction steps by creating a unified, dynamically updated model of slope stability.

proposals.title: Investigating the Coupling Between the 6-Hour Atmospheric Oscillation and Large-Scale Planetary Waves via Multi-Scale InSAR Tropospheric Delay Analysis
proposals.motivation: Understanding interactions between short-period atmospheric oscillations (e.g., the 6h wave) and large-scale planetary waves is crucial for improving atmospheric dynamics models and correcting tropospheric noise in geodetic measurements. Prior to 2026, the impact of these wave-wave interactions on the spatial and temporal structure of tropospheric water vapor, a major error source for InSAR landslide monitoring, remained poorly quantified. This research directly addresses a key uncertainty in high-precision, long-term InSAR deformation time series used for slope stability assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The amplitude and phase of the 6-hour oscillation are modulated by the passage of large-scale planetary waves (e.g., the quasi-2-day wave), creating a spatially and temporally correlated signal in InSAR-derived tropospheric delay that can be systematically characterized and modeled.
proposals.method: We will analyze multi-year Sentinel-1 InSAR stacks over large, topographically diverse regions (e.g., the Tibetan Plateau and adjacent slopes) to generate high-resolution tropospheric delay time series. Using a multi-scale decomposition approach combining Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD) and band-pass filtering, we will isolate signals in the ~6-hour and ~2-day bands. Cross-wavelet and coherence analysis will then be applied to quantify the temporal and spatial interaction between these wave modes. The analysis will be validated against co-located GNSS meteorology and ERA5 reanalysis data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical model of the interaction between the 6h oscillation and planetary waves as observed through InSAR tropospheric delays. It will advance atmospheric dynamics by providing observational constraints on multi-scale wave coupling. For the deformation monitoring community, it will lead to improved strategies for mitigating correlated atmospheric noise, enhancing the detection of subtle, long-term landslide precursors.
proposals.novelty: While InSAR is routinely used for atmospheric studies and deformation, applying multi-scale time-series analysis specifically to diagnose the interaction between these two specific wave modes in the tropospheric delay field is novel. The approach bridges Yi Luo's expertise in ground/spaceborne SAR for slopes with fundamental atmospheric dynamics, creating a new application for geodetic methods.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale SAR Constellation Optimization Framework for Global Landslide Hazard Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Current satellite constellations for Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Earth observation are not optimally designed for monitoring geohazards like landslides, which require specific temporal and spatial resolutions. The proliferation of planned commercial SAR constellations (e.g., ICEYE, Capella Space) and public missions (e.g., NISAR, Sentinel-1 Next Generation) presents an opportunity to strategically design a coordinated global system. This research addresses the critical need for a systematic framework to optimize future constellation architectures specifically for high-risk slope deformation monitoring on a global scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-objective optimization framework, integrating empirical constraints derived from multi-source SAR deformation monitoring, can generate Pareto-optimal satellite constellation configurations that significantly improve the detection and characterization of landslide precursors compared to existing or ad-hoc planned systems.
proposals.method: The method builds on the researcher's prior work fusing ground-based radar, spaceborne InSAR (e.g., Sentinel-1), and GNSS. First, we will compile a global database of landslide deformation characteristics (velocity, spatial scale, seasonality) from published studies and our own case histories (e.g., Woda). Second, we will define key performance indicators (KPIs) such as revisit time, spatial resolution, and latency. Third, we will develop a simulation and optimization engine using a genetic algorithm or particle swarm optimization to explore the trade-space of orbital parameters (altitude, inclination, phasing) and sensor specifications, minimizing cost functions based on the KPIs and the deformation database.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source simulation tool for SAR constellation design tailored to geohazard monitoring. It will deliver specific recommendations for orbital configurations and sensor priorities for future missions. The results will provide a quantitative, science-driven benchmark for evaluating commercial and public SAR constellation plans in the pre-2026 landscape.
proposals.novelty: While constellation optimization is studied for general Earth observation, this approach is novel by directly embedding geophysical constraints from multi-scale landslide deformation monitoring into the core optimization loop, moving beyond generic coverage metrics to hazard-specific performance.

proposals.title: An Integrated Real-Time Early Warning System for Landslides Using Multi-Source SAR Data Fusion and Dynamic Thresholding
proposals.motivation: Landslides cause significant loss of life and economic damage globally. Existing monitoring often relies on periodic satellite InSAR or post-event analysis, lacking the temporal resolution for true early warning. The integration of continuous, high-frequency ground-based radar (GB-SAR) with wide-area, systematic satellite SAR (e.g., Sentinel-1) into a real-time operational system remains a critical, unsolved challenge in pre-2026 geohazard management.
proposals.hypothesis: A data fusion framework that intelligently integrates real-time GB-SAR displacement streams with periodically updated satellite InSAR-derived velocity fields can reliably detect acceleration precursors and generate timely warnings with lower false alarm rates than single-sensor systems.
proposals.method: The system will employ a modular architecture: 1) A near-real-time processing chain for GB-SAR using Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) on sliding windows. 2) Automated ingestion and processing of Sentinel-1 data via the SBAS-InSAR method to update regional deformation baselines. 3) A fusion core based on a Kalman Filter to assimilate both data streams, reconciling their different spatial footprints and temporal sampling. 4) A dynamic, site-adaptive thresholding algorithm that triggers warnings based on fused displacement rates and acceleration trends, accounting for meteorological triggers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully operational prototype early warning system, validated on a known unstable slope. It will provide a concrete methodology for multi-source SAR data fusion in real-time, moving beyond retrospective analysis. The results will establish a new benchmark for integrating spatial context from satellites with temporal detail from ground systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, operational fusion architecture and the dynamic thresholding model specifically designed for the complementary GB-SAR/spaceborne InSAR data pair, which is not addressed by existing offline, multi-temporal analysis studies.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Validation of 3D Landslide Deformation Models: Integrating Dense GNSS Networks with Multi-Geometry InSAR and GB-SAR
proposals.motivation: Accurate 3D deformation models are critical for understanding landslide mechanics and improving early warning systems. However, current validation relies on sparse in-situ measurements, which cannot resolve the high spatial variability of displacement within a landslide body, leading to significant uncertainty in model reliability and geohazard assessment. This gap limits the operational use of advanced InSAR and ground-based radar (GB-SAR) techniques for precise slope stability evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: A strategically densified network of continuous GNSS stations will provide a high-resolution 3D ground-truth dataset that reveals significant spatial heterogeneity in landslide motion, enabling a more rigorous and spatially explicit validation of integrated InSAR/GB-SAR 3D decomposition results than previously possible.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense network of 15-20 low-cost, continuous GNSS stations across the Woda landslide, supplementing existing sparse networks. 3D surface displacement will be derived by integrating ascending/descending Sentinel-1 InSAR time series (processed via SBAS or PSI algorithms) and multi-geometry GB-SAR data (using the Slope-SAR method for 3D decomposition). The high-resolution GNSS 3D vectors will be used to quantitatively validate the radar-derived 3D displacement fields at the station locations, employing statistical metrics (RMSE, correlation) and spatially interpolated error mapping to identify zones of model misfit.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a benchmark dataset and validation framework for high-resolution 3D landslide monitoring, directly quantifying the accuracy and limitations of integrated radar-based 3D modeling. It will provide actionable guidelines for optimal sensor deployment and data fusion, advancing the transition from qualitative displacement mapping to quantitatively validated 3D kinematic models for slope disaster management.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor fusion is established, the systematic validation of the fused 3D output using a purpose-deployed, dense GNSS network tailored to capture intra-landslide deformation gradients represents a significant advance beyond point-wise or sparse validation common in pre-2026 studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Hydrological Trigger Mechanisms for Landslide Acceleration Using Multi-Sensor 3D Deformation Time Series
proposals.motivation: Landslide early warning remains a significant challenge due to the complex, site-specific interplay between rainfall, pore pressure, and slope deformation. Pre-2026 studies often correlate rainfall with 1D line-of-sight displacement, but the full 3D deformation vector and its temporal relationship with subsurface hydrology are critical for understanding failure initiation. This research directly addresses the need for a mechanistic, data-driven model linking hydrological forcing to the complete 3D kinematic response of unstable slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: That the transition from steady-state creep to accelerated landslide motion is triggered by specific, quantifiable thresholds in the phase lag and magnitude of 3D deformation responses to rainfall infiltration and groundwater level fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-frequency GB-SAR (ground-based radar) and multi-geometry Sentinel-1 InSAR time series to derive daily-scale 3D deformation vectors for a well-instrumented landslide (e.g., Woda). These will be correlated with in-situ rainfall and piezometric data. The analysis will employ a multivariate Vector Autoregression (VAR) model and cross-wavelet coherence to quantify lead-lag relationships and identify deformation response thresholds. A physics-informed neural network (PINN) will then be trained to model the coupled hydro-mechanical triggering process.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable framework for modeling hydrological landslide triggers based on integrated 3D deformation monitoring. It will deliver quantitative thresholds for early warning and improve the physical basis for predicting acceleration phases. The methodology advances the fusion of multi-sensor SAR and hydrological data for slope stability assessment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit use of high-resolution 3D deformation vectors (not just 1D LOS) as the primary response variable for trigger modeling, combined with the application of VAR and PINN architectures to disentangle and quantify the complex, time-lagged causal relationships between hydrological inputs and slope kinematics.

proposals.title: Automated Detection and Spatiotemporal Tracking of Precursory Cracks for Landslide Early Warning by Fusing UAV Optical Imagery and Multi-Temporal InSAR Displacement Data
proposals.motivation: Surface cracks are critical precursors for landslide failure, but their manual detection over large, hazardous slopes is inefficient and subjective. While UAVs and InSAR provide complementary high-resolution spatial and temporal deformation data, pre-2026 methods lack robust, automated workflows to directly link distributed surface cracking from imagery with subsurface deformation patterns from radar, limiting the ability to mechanistically understand and forecast slope instability.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-source data fusion framework, combining deep learning-based crack detection on UAV orthophotos with time-series InSAR displacement fields, can automatically identify and track the evolution of crack networks and correlate their kinematics with deep-seated deformation mechanisms, providing a quantifiable proxy for accelerating slope failure.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, a U-Net or DeepLabv3+ convolutional neural network, pre-trained on natural scene crack datasets and fine-tuned with labeled UAV orthophotos, will segment crack pixels. Second, detected cracks will be vectorized and registered with time-series InSAR (e.g., SBAS or PSI) deformation maps and coherence data. We will implement tracking using optical flow and feature matching across UAV epochs, while a statistical fusion model will correlate crack propagation vectors (length, density, orientation) with localized InSAR displacement rates and gradients to identify causative relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, automated pipeline for fusing optical and radar remote sensing to extract precursory fracture features. It will advance quantitative landslide precursor analysis by establishing empirical and mechanistic links between surface crack evolution and subsurface deformation, directly enhancing the physical basis for early warning systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated spatiotemporal tracking of discrete crack features (from UAV) within a continuous deformation field (from InSAR), moving beyond separate analyses to a coupled kinematic model. This addresses a key gap in pre-2026 slope stability monitoring, which typically treats surface and deep-seated indicators independently.

proposals.title: Enhanced Dense Displacement Field Retrieval for Landslide Monitoring by Integrating Optical Flow Algorithms with SAR Offset Tracking
proposals.motivation: Accurate, dense displacement fields are critical for understanding landslide kinematics and early warning. Traditional SAR offset tracking methods, while valuable, often produce sparse or noisy results, especially in areas with low coherence or complex non-linear motion. This limits the detailed analysis of deformation patterns needed for robust slope stability assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced optical flow algorithms, specifically designed for dense motion estimation in computer vision, can be adapted to process SAR azimuth and range offsets, yielding denser, more accurate, and spatially continuous 2D displacement maps than conventional cross-correlation-based offset tracking.
proposals.method: We will adapt state-of-the-art deep learning optical flow architectures (e.g., PWC-Net or RAFT, pre-2026 standards) to learn the mapping between SAR amplitude image pairs and sub-pixel displacement fields. The network will be trained on synthetic datasets generated from realistic deformation models and real SAR data with known offsets. The core innovation is formulating the SAR offset tracking problem as a supervised learning task for dense regression, using both range and azimuth amplitude images as input channels. Performance will be validated against ground-based InSAR (GB-SAR) and GNSS measurements from known landslide sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source tool for generating high-resolution landslide displacement maps. It will advance the fusion of computer vision and radar remote sensing, offering a significant improvement in spatial detail over existing offset-tracking methods. The resulting dense fields will enable more precise identification of landslide boundaries, internal strain patterns, and kinematic units.
proposals.novelty: While optical flow is established in computer vision, its direct application to SAR amplitude images for geophysical displacement retrieval is not standard. The novelty lies in tailoring and training these advanced algorithms specifically for the noise characteristics and geometric properties of SAR data, moving beyond simple cross-correlation or phase-based methods.

proposals.title: A Data Assimilation Framework for Landslide Forecasting: Integrating Multi-Sensor Deformation Data into a Time-Dependent Physical Model
proposals.motivation: Current landslide early warning relies heavily on empirical thresholds of displacement velocity, which often fail to account for the complex, time-varying physical processes driving slope failure. Integrating disparate deformation measurements from ground-based radar, InSAR, and GNSS into a unified predictive model remains a significant challenge, limiting the accuracy and lead time of warnings. This research addresses this gap by adapting sophisticated data assimilation techniques, proven in numerical weather prediction, to the geotechnical domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that assimilating multi-sensor deformation data (GB-SAR, InSAR, GNSS) into a time-dependent visco-plastic slope model using an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) will significantly improve the model's state estimation and enable more accurate short-term forecasts of landslide acceleration and failure timing than using any single data source or heuristic thresholds.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular data assimilation framework. The physical model will be a 1D or 2D visco-plastic creep law, parameterized by shear strength and viscosity, driven by pore-pressure changes from meteorological data. The EnKF will be implemented to sequentially update the model's state vector (displacement, velocity, model parameters) by integrating observations from TerraSAR-X/Sentinel-1 InSAR, ground-based IBIS-SAR, and GNSS. The system will be tested and validated using historical data from the well-instrumented Woda landslide, comparing forecasts against actual failure events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-informed operational framework for landslide forecasting that moves beyond displacement monitoring to predictive simulation. It will provide a methodological blueprint for fusing asynchronous, multi-scale SAR and geodetic data. The resulting open-source tool will enable a quantitative, probabilistic assessment of slope stability evolution, directly contributing to improved early warning systems.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is standard in meteorology and oceanography, its application to integrate multi-sensor deformation data into a time-dependent geomechanical model for landslide forecasting is novel. The proposed EnKF approach explicitly handles model and observation uncertainties to dynamically correct both the model state and its key physical parameters, a significant advance over static calibration or simple trend analysis.

proposals.title: High-Frequency Ground-Based SAR Monitoring of Individual Crack Kinematics for Precursory Slope Failure Analysis
proposals.motivation: Precursory deformation of landslides often localizes along discrete cracks, but current InSAR and GB-SAR monitoring typically averages displacement over meters to tens of meters, obscuring critical millimeter-scale crack propagation mechanics. Understanding the kinematic evolution of single cracks is essential for developing physics-based early warning models. This gap limits the ability to predict the transition from localized damage to catastrophic slope failure.
proposals.hypothesis: High-frequency (e.g., Ku-band) GB-SAR, with sub-millimeter precision, can resolve the opening, shear, and propagation dynamics of individual slope cracks, and that these dynamics exhibit characteristic temporal patterns preceding major failure events.
proposals.method: A high-frequency (e.g., 17.2 GHz) GB-SAR system will be deployed for continuous monitoring of a known unstable slope with pre-existing cracks. A tailored processing chain will be implemented, combining Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) on natural reflectors and Amplitude-Based Offset Tracking on artificial corner reflectors placed near crack tips. Time-series displacement will be analyzed at the sub-crack scale using a multi-temporal 3D decomposition model integrating LOS data from multiple GB-SAR viewing geometries to isolate crack opening versus shear components.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for quantifying single-crack displacement fields, providing unprecedented data on pre-failure crack kinematics. It will contribute a new geotechnical parameter—crack propagation rate—for integration into slope stability models and early warning systems, moving beyond area-averaged displacement thresholds.
proposals.novelty: While GB-SAR is used for slope monitoring, its application is shifted from area-wide deformation to the targeted, millimeter-scale kinematics of individual fracture propagation, a scale typically addressed only by point sensors or manual crack meters, not by radar remote sensing.

proposals.title: Inversion of Multi-Sensor 3D Deformation Fields for Geotechnical Property Estimation in Landslide Materials
proposals.motivation: Current landslide monitoring using InSAR and ground-based radar provides high-resolution deformation data but treats geotechnical properties as static inputs. Inverting deformation to estimate these properties is crucial for understanding failure mechanisms and improving stability models. This approach can transform monitoring from descriptive to predictive, enabling more accurate hazard assessment and early warning.
proposals.hypothesis: The 3D deformation field of a landslide, derived from the synergistic integration of spaceborne InSAR and ground-based radar, can be inverted using a constrained optimization framework to yield spatially distributed estimates of key geotechnical parameters, such as cohesion and internal friction angle.
proposals.method: We will first generate a time-series 3D deformation field by fusing ascending/descending Sentinel-1 InSAR data with GB-SAR data, using methods like the SBAS technique and 3D decomposition. This deformation field will serve as input to a finite element model (FEM) of the slope. We will then employ a Bayesian inversion framework (e.g., using Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling) or a gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm) to iteratively adjust the material properties in the FEM until the simulated deformation matches the observed field, constrained by prior information from limited in-situ tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel methodology to quantitatively map geotechnical property variations within a landslide body using remote sensing data alone. It will provide a framework for calibrating numerical models with spatially continuous data, moving beyond point-based measurements. The results will offer new insights into material heterogeneity and its control on landslide kinematics.
proposals.novelty: While deformation inversion is common in tectonics, its application to landslide-scale problems for geotechnical property estimation is nascent. The novelty lies in the tight coupling of multi-sensor 3D deformation products with a probabilistic inversion scheme specifically tailored for slope-scale, heterogeneous materials.

proposals.title: Calibrating Landslide Runout Models via Multi-Temporal InSAR and GB-SAR Post-Failure Deformation Patterns
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of landslide runout distance and deposition geometry is critical for hazard zonation and risk assessment, yet numerical models (e.g., DAN3D, MassMov2D) rely heavily on poorly constrained rheological parameters. Pre-2026, the calibration of these models has primarily used final deposit maps, neglecting the valuable kinematic information encoded in the deformation field during the runout and immediate post-failure settlement phase.
proposals.hypothesis: That the spatio-temporal deformation patterns of a landslide deposit, measured by Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) techniques, provide a robust, multi-dimensional dataset to inversely calibrate the frictional and rheological parameters of runout models, leading to more accurate and physically representative simulations.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-failure (spaceborne InSAR) and high-resolution post-failure (Ground-Based SAR) deformation time series for a well-documented landslide. The final deposit boundary and digital elevation model (DEM) of difference will provide primary constraints. We will then use the observed post-failure surface deformation velocity field and its temporal decay as secondary calibration targets. A Bayesian inversion framework will be employed to optimize the parameters (e.g., Voellmy friction coefficients, Bingham viscosity) of a depth-averaged continuum model (e.g., DAN3D) by minimizing the misfit between simulated and observed deposition geometry AND the post-failure deformation patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for runout model calibration that moves beyond static geometric fitting to incorporate dynamic deformation data. It will produce better-constrained, more reliable runout models for hazard analysis and provide new insights into the consolidation and stabilization processes of landslide deposits.
proposals.novelty: While InSAR/GB-SAR are established for pre-failure monitoring, their use as a quantitative dataset for calibrating the *dynamic* parameters of runout models via inversion of post-failure deformation is a novel and untapped approach, creating a direct link between geodetic observation and numerical simulation in the post-failure regime.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale SAR Integration Framework for Quantifying Kinematics of Rock Glaciers and Earthflows in High Mountain Asia
proposals.motivation: Rock glaciers and slow-moving earthflows are widespread periglacial and geomorphic processes in high mountains, representing significant geohazards and freshwater stores. Pre-2026, their kinematics are poorly quantified at regional scales due to complex, non-linear motion patterns and the limitations of single-sensor observations. An integrated monitoring approach is needed to understand their sensitivity to climate forcing and improve hazard assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The synergistic integration of spaceborne InSAR (for wide-area coverage and long-term trends) and ground-based radar (for high-temporal-resolution, 3D deformation) can successfully resolve the full spatiotemporal deformation patterns of rock glaciers and earthflows, revealing distinct seasonal and multi-year kinematic regimes.
proposals.method: We will apply Persistent Scatterer InSAR (PSI) and Small Baseline Subset (SBAS) algorithms to Sentinel-1 and ALOS-2/PALSAR-2 time series to map long-term, wide-area surface velocities. For selected key sites, we will deploy a ground-based real-aperture radar (GB-RAR, e.g., IBIS-L) to acquire sub-daily displacement time series. A novel data fusion algorithm, based on a Kalman filter framework, will be developed to integrate the multi-source SAR datasets, GNSS, and meteorological data (temperature, precipitation) to generate a unified 4D (3D + time) deformation model and attribute motion phases to environmental drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first multi-sensor, validated kinematic models for diverse mass movement types beyond classic landslides. It will deliver a scalable processing workflow and open-source code for SAR data fusion. The results will provide critical empirical data to calibrate and improve thermomechanical models of permafrost-related creep and earthflow dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While integrated SAR is established for landslides, its systematic application to the distinct rheology and signal characteristics of rock glaciers and earthflows is novel. The development of a dedicated fusion algorithm to reconcile the different spatial footprints and temporal sampling of GB-SAR and spaceborne InSAR for these processes represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Dynamic Spectral-Guided Spatiotemporal Fusion of Geostationary and Multispectral Data for High-Refresh Landslide Precursor Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Landslide early warning requires monitoring subtle, rapid changes in soil moisture and vegetation stress, which are best captured by the Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR) band. However, existing high-spatial-resolution SWIR data (e.g., from Sentinel-2) has a low revisit rate (5 days), missing critical short-term dynamics. High-temporal-resolution geostationary data (e.g., from Himawari-8/9 or GOES-R) provides frequent observations but at coarse spatial scales, limiting its use for precise slope-scale analysis. A robust downscaling method is urgently needed to generate high-resolution, high-refresh SWIR composites for monitoring unstable slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive downscaling framework that dynamically selects the optimal high-resolution spectral band as a guide, based on real-time correlation analysis with the coarser SWIR signal under prevailing illumination and atmospheric conditions, will produce more accurate and temporally consistent high-resolution SWIR data than static band-selection methods.
proposals.method: We will fuse high-refresh (10-min) geostationary AHI (Himawari) SWIR data with high-resolution (20m) Sentinel-2 multispectral data. A dynamic selection algorithm will analyze the temporal stream of geostationary data to identify, for each fusion epoch, the Sentinel-2 band (e.g., Green, Red, Red-Edge, NIR) whose temporal variation best correlates with the SWIR signal over a spatially matched training area. This selected guide band will then drive a spatiotemporal fusion model (e.g., an enhanced Fit-FC or STARFM algorithm) to downscale the geostationary SWIR data, ensuring the guiding proxy and target SWIR observations are temporally aligned within a two-hour window.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, condition-aware data fusion framework that generates synthetic high-spatial-temporal-resolution SWIR time series. It will directly enhance the capability for near-real-time monitoring of soil water content and vegetation health on slopes, providing a new data layer for integration into multi-sensor landslide early warning systems that combine InSAR, GB-SAR, and GNSS.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the dynamic, state-dependent selection of the guiding spectral band for fusion, moving beyond the static use of Red or NIR bands. This adaptive approach explicitly minimizes the temporal decorrelation between the guide proxy and the target SWIR signal, a critical limitation in existing spatiotemporal fusion methods for monitoring rapidly evolving ground conditions.

proposals.title: Harmonizing Sentinel-2 Spectral Resolution via PSF-Informed Upscaling for Enhanced Landslide Precursor Detection
proposals.motivation: Multi-spectral Sentinel-2 data is crucial for detecting pre-failure landslide indicators like vegetation stress and moisture changes. However, its native multi-resolution bands (10m, 20m, 60m) introduce spatial misregistration and spectral mixing artifacts when fused for time-series analysis, degrading the reliability of deformation-correlation studies. A robust, physically consistent harmonization method is needed to improve the quality of fused optical data used alongside InSAR and ground-based radar for slope stability assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Upscaling all Sentinel-2 bands to a uniform 20m resolution using a method explicitly informed by the sensor's Point Spread Function (PSF) will produce a more physically coherent multi-spectral dataset, thereby improving the accuracy of machine learning classifiers in identifying landslide precursors compared to using natively resampled or naively interpolated data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PSF-informed upscaling framework from the seismic imaging domain. For each Sentinel-2 band, we will model its specific PSF using pre-launch characterization data and apply a convolutional super-resolution approach (e.g., using a deep residual network) to upsample 10m and 60m bands to 20m, explicitly accounting for the information loss and blurring inherent to the sensor. The harmonized dataset will be used to train a Random Forest or CNN classifier to detect spectral precursors within known deformation areas mapped by GB-SAR and InSAR from Yi Luo's previous studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, open-source processing workflow for generating PSF-aware, harmonized Sentinel-2 time series optimized for geohazard monitoring. It will demonstrate a tangible improvement in landslide precursor classification accuracy, strengthening the integration of optical and radar remote sensing for early warning systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the geophysical concept of dataset harmonization via intentional resolution coarsening for model stability to optical remote sensing, and in implementing a PSF-informed upscaling method—common in astronomy—for operational landslide monitoring, moving beyond simple bilinear or cubic interpolation.

proposals.title: A Geostationary-Guided Spatiotemporal Fusion Framework for High-Resolution, Near-Real-Time Landslide Precursor Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Rapid slope deformation, often triggered by intense rainfall, is a critical precursor to catastrophic landslides. Existing satellite monitoring faces a trade-off: spaceborne InSAR provides high spatial resolution but suffers from long revisit times and decorrelation during fast events, while geostationary satellites offer continuous temporal sampling but lack the spatial detail to resolve small-scale slope instabilities. A method to generate high-resolution, high-frequency surface change indices is urgently needed for early warning.
proposals.hypothesis: That the dense temporal signal from geostationary satellites (e.g., Himawari-8) can serve as a dynamic prior to guide the spatial downscaling and temporal interpolation of Sentinel-2-derived indices (like NDVI and NDWI), enabling the reconstruction of landslide-relevant environmental indices at both high spatial (10m) and temporal (10-min) resolution to detect precursory hydrological and vegetation stress.
proposals.method: We will adapt a deep learning-based spatiotemporal fusion architecture (inspired by pre-2026 work on STARFM and FSDAF). The model will use paired Sentinel-2 (10-20m) and co-registered Himawari-8 (500m-1km) data for training. Sentinel-2 will provide high-resolution targets for indices using its red-edge and SWIR bands. The network will learn to map the temporal evolution of the geostationary data stream, guided by spatial details from sparse Sentinel-2 acquisitions, to produce a continuous 10m resolution time series. Validation will use ground-based radar and GNSS data from known landslide sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel operational framework for generating landslide precursor indices (soil moisture, vegetation stress) at an unprecedented spatiotemporal resolution. It directly extends the researcher's expertise in multi-sensor fusion for slope stability to the domain of near-real-time, high-resolution environmental monitoring, providing a new tool for disaster early warning systems.
proposals.novelty: While spatiotemporal fusion exists, using geostationary data not merely for gap-filling but as a dynamic prior to downscale specific Sentinel-2 band combinations for geohazard monitoring is novel. This moves beyond land cover change to focus on the high-frequency dynamics of physically meaningful indices for slope stability assessment.

proposals.title: A Multi-Landscape Validation of Pan-Sharpening Downscaling for Enhanced InSAR Deformation Monitoring of Slopes
proposals.motivation: Pan-sharpening methods are used to downscale coarse-resolution Sentinel-1 InSAR deformation data using high-resolution optical imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2), aiming to improve spatial detail for landslide monitoring. However, the performance of these data fusion techniques is highly dependent on landscape characteristics and the presence of water bodies, which can degrade radar coherence. A systematic evaluation across diverse terrains is lacking, limiting confidence in applying these methods for operational slope stability assessment in varied geographical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy and accuracy of pan-sharpening downscaling for InSAR-derived slope deformation maps are not uniform but vary systematically with landscape type (e.g., vegetated mountains, arid badlands, urbanized slopes) and the extent of water bodies, with optimal performance in coherent, texturally rich, and dry environments.
proposals.method: We will select multiple test sites in China representing distinct landscapes: steep vegetated slopes (Three Gorges), arid loess regions, and urbanized hillslopes. For each, we will apply established pan-sharpening algorithms (e.g., Principal Component Analysis, Gram-Schmidt, and Deep Learning-based SRCNN as of pre-2026) to fuse Sentinel-1 Interferometric Wide Swath data with Sentinel-2 multispectral bands. The downscaled deformation velocity maps will be rigorously validated against high-resolution ground-truth data from Yi Luo's previous work, specifically Ground-Based SAR (GB-SAR) and dense GNSS measurements. Performance metrics (RMSE, structural similarity index, coherence preservation) will be quantified and correlated with landscape descriptors (NDVI, slope, water mask).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, quantitative guidelines on where and when pan-sharpening downscaling is reliable for slope deformation monitoring, directly informing best practices for InSAR-based landslide hazard assessment. It will extend the researcher's expertise in multi-sensor fusion by establishing the geographical boundaries of a promising downscaling technique.
proposals.novelty: While pan-sharpening itself is a known technique, its targeted application and validation for InSAR slope deformation products across a controlled gradient of real-world geographical conditions is novel. The direct benchmarking against GB-SAR—a premium validation dataset—in diverse landscapes moves beyond single-case-study demonstrations.

proposals.title: Super-Resolution Fusion of Multi-Sensor SAR and Optical Data for Enhanced Landslide Deformation Monitoring
proposals.motivation: High-resolution surface deformation data is critical for precise landslide hazard assessment but is limited by the native resolution of individual satellite sensors. The growing volume of data from constellations like Sentinel-1 (SAR) and Sentinel-2/Landsat (optical) provides multiple, slightly offset observations of the same slope over short periods. Current fusion methods often fail to fully exploit the sub-pixel spatial and complementary information in these multi-sensor, multi-temporal datasets to synthetically enhance spatial resolution for deformation tracking.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the stochastic localization and reconstruction principles of super-resolution microscopy (e.g., STORM/PALM) to satellite remote sensing, we can fuse multiple medium-resolution, spatiotemporally offset satellite images to generate a synthetically enhanced, high-resolution composite image, thereby improving the spatial detail of derived deformation fields for landslide monitoring.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel processing chain for landslide sites. First, we will co-register and preprocess a time series of Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 optical data over a known unstable slope, leveraging sub-pixel offsets from orbital geometries. We will then adapt the core algorithm of super-resolution optical fluctuation imaging (SOFI) or a Bayesian localization framework to treat each satellite pass as a stochastic sample. The method will localize persistent scatterers (from SAR) and key terrain features (from optical data) across these samples, iteratively reconstructing a higher-resolution grid. The output will be validated against high-resolution ground-based InSAR and GNSS data from Yi Luo's previous studies.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a new computational framework for downscaling satellite constellation data, moving beyond simple stacking or pan-sharpening. It will provide a methodology to generate enhanced-resolution displacement time series for landslides, improving the detection of small-scale precursory movements.
