The work will bridge a conceptual gap between microscopy and geospatial analysis, offering a new tool for multi-sensor disaster monitoring.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual translation of stochastic super-resolution microscopy techniques to the domain of multi-sensor satellite remote sensing is novel. Unlike traditional image fusion, this approach explicitly models and utilizes the stochastic sampling inherent in temporally close satellite passes to achieve a synthetic resolution enhancement, which has not been systematically explored for geohazard applications.

proposals.title: A Multi-Sensor Downscaling Framework for Landslide Precursor Detection Using Landsat 8/9 and Sentinel-2 Data
proposals.motivation: Current InSAR-based landslide monitoring is often constrained by the spatial resolution of freely available SAR data (e.g., Sentinel-1's ~20m), which can obscure localized pre-failure deformation signals. While downscaling methods using very high-resolution (VHR) optical data exist, they are not routinely applied to operational, moderate-resolution satellite constellations. Leveraging the rich, multi-spectral archives of Landsat 8/9 and Sentinel-2 offers a promising but underexplored path to enhance deformation detail for regional-scale slope stability assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: That a data fusion and downscaling approach, combining the temporal density of Sentinel-1 InSAR time series with the high spatial and spectral information of Landsat 8/9 and Sentinel-2, can generate enhanced, meter-scale displacement maps that reveal precursor deformation patterns not discernible in standard InSAR products.
proposals.method: The method will integrate SBAS-InSAR-derived displacement time series from Sentinel-1 with spatially coincident, multi-temporal stacks of Landsat 8/9 and Sentinel-2 optical data. A Random Forest or a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) regression model will be trained to learn the non-linear relationship between low-resolution InSAR displacement and high-resolution optical features (e.g., NDVI, texture, spectral indices). The trained model will then be applied to downscale the InSAR displacement maps, validated against ground-based radar and GNSS measurements from known landslide sites in China.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, open-data methodology for generating enhanced deformation products, improving the spatial acuity of regional landslide hazard maps. It will demonstrate the operational value of synergizing legacy (Landsat) and Copernicus (Sentinel) constellations for geohazard monitoring, directly supporting disaster risk reduction strategies.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling has been explored with single VHR images, this proposal systematically applies it to the continuous, multi-year data streams of operational moderate-resolution constellations (Landsat & Sentinel-2) specifically for landslide deformation enhancement, moving the concept from a case-study tool towards a monitoring service.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Spatiotemporal Fusion Network for Generating Gap-Free, High-Resolution Global Water Dynamics from Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2, and Landsat 8
proposals.motivation: High-resolution (10-m) optical water indices like MNDWI from Sentinel-2 are critical for monitoring floods, droughts, and wetland dynamics, but persistent cloud cover creates severe data gaps, especially in tropical and monsoon regions. Existing gap-filling methods using lower-resolution MODIS or simple interpolation lack the spatial detail or physical consistency needed for operational disaster assessment and hydrological modeling. A robust, globally applicable system to produce daily, cloud-free 10-m water maps remains an unsolved operational challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep learning architecture that jointly learns from the temporal context of optical indices and the all-weather backscatter signals from Sentinel-1 SAR, constrained by physical rules of water dynamics, can accurately impute missing high-resolution water values and generate a globally consistent, gap-free daily time series.
proposals.method: We will develop a U-Net-based spatiotemporal transformer model. It ingests multi-temporal stacks of Sentinel-2 MNDWI, Sentinel-1 VV/VH backscatter, and Landsat 8 SWIR bands, with cloud masks and auxiliary data. A dedicated SAR encoder will extract hydrological features (e.g., flood edges, soil moisture). The model will be trained using a physically-guided loss function that penalizes violations of known constraints (e.g., water persistence, topographic boundaries from SRTM). We will implement a novel adversarial training regime where cloud masks are artificially applied to clear scenes to create a supervised learning task for imputation, with uncertainty quantified via Monte Carlo dropout.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first operational framework for generating a global, daily, 10-m water dynamics product with quantified per-pixel uncertainty. It will provide a foundational dataset for high-resolution flood inundation mapping, coastal change analysis, and drought impact assessment in cloudy regions. The methodology advances multi-sensor fusion by embedding physical rules directly into the deep learning loss function.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a physics-guided loss function within a spatiotemporal deep learning model for water imputation is novel, moving beyond purely data-driven fusion. Furthermore, the specific adversarial training strategy using synthetic cloud masks on optical-SAR data pairs to create a perfect reconstruction benchmark has not been extensively applied to global water mapping at this scale.

proposals.title: Automated Anisotropic Downscaling of Global Water Data: A Fusion Framework for High-Resolution Extent and Flux Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Global surface water monitoring is critical for understanding hydrological cycles, flood hazards, and coastal dynamics, yet existing operational products like the Global Surface Water dataset (Pekel et al., 2016) or MODIS-based water masks are limited to 30-500m resolution, obscuring small water bodies and fine-scale dynamics. High-resolution (<10m) commercial SAR and optical data (e.g., Sentinel-1/2) offer detail but lack automated, globally consistent methods to derive dynamic water flux vectors, which are essential for quantifying water movement in events like flash floods or tidal inundation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fusing coarse-resolution global water time series with high-resolution Sentinel-2 imagery via an automated, scene-specific anisotropic downscaling model can simultaneously generate accurate 10-m water extent maps and directional flux vectors, capturing localized hydrodynamic processes not resolvable at native coarse scales.
proposals.method: The pipeline first employs a deep learning-based super-resolution model (e.g., Enhanced Deep Residual Networks, EDSR) trained on Sentinel-2 MNDWI and Landsat data to downscale 30m Landsat-derived water masks to a consistent 10m global time series. For each high-resolution scene, a feature extraction module (using pre-2026 CNNs like ResNet) will classify spatiotemporal patterns (e.g., linear features for rivers, radial patterns for floods). Based on this classification, an anisotropic model selection algorithm will choose an appropriate directional filter (e.g., gradient-based for tidal flow, directional morphological for riverbanks) from a pre-defined library to compute pixel-wise water flux vectors from the time series.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first operational, automated pipeline for generating concurrent global 10-m water extent and flux vector products, directly enhancing fine-scale hydrological and hazard monitoring. It will provide a novel data fusion framework that bridges the gap between static water mapping and dynamic process modeling, offering critical insights for landslide-triggering rainfall-runoff analysis and coastal erosion studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of deep learning-based downscaling with automated, scene-specific anisotropic model selection for flux calculation, moving beyond static water mapping to dynamic vector generation at an operational global scale—a capability not present in pre-2026 water remote sensing literature.

proposals.title: Optimizing Multi-Scale InSAR Deformation Monitoring for Landslide Detection: A Systematic Resolution Degradation Approach
proposals.motivation: In landslide monitoring, the choice of spatial resolution in InSAR data significantly impacts the detection and characterization of deformation signals. High-resolution data can be noisy and computationally expensive, while low-resolution data may miss critical small-scale precursors to failure. There is a lack of a systematic framework to determine the optimal resolution scale for reliable, automated landslide change detection, hindering efficient early warning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given landslide type and geomorphic setting, there exists an optimal, coarsened spatial resolution that maximizes the signal-to-noise ratio of deformation time series, enabling more robust automated detection than using native high-resolution data.
proposals.method: The method will leverage Dr. Luo's expertise by applying systematic spatial degradation (averaging and decimation) to high-resolution ground-based (GB-SAR) and spaceborne (e.g., Sentinel-1) SAR datasets from known landslide sites. We will generate multi-resolution deformation stacks using Persistent Scatterer (PS) and Small Baseline Subset (SBAS) InSAR techniques. Automated change detection will be performed at each scale using statistical thresholding and time-series clustering algorithms. The optimal scale will be identified by maximizing metrics like the Matthews Correlation Coefficient against validated ground truth deformation maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, data-driven protocol for selecting cost-effective InSAR resolutions for landslide monitoring programs. It will contribute a novel multi-scale analysis framework to the InSAR community, moving beyond ad-hoc resolution choices. The results will inform the design of future monitoring strategies, balancing detection accuracy with data volume and processing demands.
proposals.novelty: While multi-resolution analysis is common in remote sensing, the systematic, inverse approach of degrading high-resolution InSAR data to find an optimal *coarser* scale for landslide detection is novel. This inverts the typical pursuit of highest resolution and directly addresses the trade-off between detail and operational robustness in deformation monitoring.

proposals.title: Integrating Satellite-Derived Land Surface Temperature with Multi-Temporal InSAR for Enhanced Landslide Precursor Detection and Mechanism Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Yi Luo and others has demonstrated the value of combining ground-based and spaceborne radar (InSAR) for slope deformation monitoring. However, deformation is often a lagging indicator. Land surface temperature (LST) from satellites like Landsat-8, Sentinel-3, or MODIS can provide early warning signals related to soil moisture changes, pore-water pressure fluctuations, and thermal stress, which are key preparatory factors for landslides. Integrating these datasets can move monitoring from reactive to more predictive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synergistic analysis of time-series InSAR deformation and high-resolution satellite LST data will reveal statistically significant thermal precursors to slope failure, improving the timeliness and physical understanding of landslide early warning systems.
proposals.method: We will acquire multi-temporal Sentinel-1 SAR data and apply Persistent Scatterer InSAR (PSI) and Small Baseline Subset (SBAS) algorithms to generate high-resolution deformation time series for selected landslide-prone areas in China. Concurrently, we will process Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-3 SLSTR data to derive LST time series using established split-window or single-channel algorithms. The core technical integration will involve a multivariate statistical analysis (e.g., cross-correlation, Granger causality testing) and a machine learning framework (e.g., Random Forest or Long Short-Term Memory network) to model the relationship between thermal anomalies, precipitation records, and subsequent deformation rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel multi-physics data fusion framework for landslide hazard assessment. It is expected to produce a methodology for identifying thermally anomalous 'hotspots' that precede measurable slope movement, thereby extending the forecast lead time. The results will also offer new insights into the hydro-thermal-mechanical coupling processes driving slope instability.
proposals.novelty: While InSAR and thermal remote sensing are mature individually, their systematic integration for landslide precursor analysis is not yet standard. The novelty lies in the quantitative, time-series-based coupling of these two distinct physical parameters (temperature and displacement) within a unified early-warning analytical model, moving beyond simple visual comparison.

proposals.title: Assimilating Multi-Source SAR Deformation Data into a Physics-Based Slope Stability Model Using an Ensemble Kalman Filter
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 landslide hazard assessment is limited by the spatial-temporal gaps between high-frequency ground-based radar (GB-SAR) point measurements and wide-area, low-frequency satellite InSAR observations. Integrating these disparate data streams into a unified, physically consistent model of slope deformation remains a significant challenge, hindering accurate early warning and stability evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that data assimilation (DA) techniques, specifically the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), can optimally merge sparse, high-frequency GB-SAR time series with spatially dense but temporally sparse satellite InSAR displacement fields to produce a dynamically updated and more accurate 3D deformation model of an unstable slope.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled modeling and DA framework. A simplified physics-based model (e.g., a visco-plastic creep law) will serve as the dynamic forecast model. An EnKF will be implemented to sequentially assimilate deformation measurements from both GB-SAR and multi-temporal InSAR (e.g., using SBAS or PSI techniques from Sentinel-1). The filter will update the model state (displacement and velocity fields) and key parameters, correcting the forecast with each new observation cycle. The system will be tested on a well-instrumented landslide, such as the Woda landslide, using historical data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel operational framework for fusing multi-source SAR data into a dynamic slope stability model, moving beyond comparative analysis to integrated system state estimation. It will demonstrate a pathway towards near-real-time, model-informed landslide monitoring that leverages the complementary strengths of ground-based and satellite radar systems.
proposals.novelty: While DA is standard in meteorology, its application to integrate GB-SAR and InSAR for geotechnical slope modeling is novel. The proposed method goes beyond simple data fusion or correlation, enabling the estimation of unobserved model states and parameters through a physically constrained assimilation process.

proposals.title: A Quality-Weighted, Context-Aware Fusion Framework for Robust Surface Deformation Estimation from Multi-Temporal SAR Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate estimation of surface deformation from SAR data is critical for landslide and slope stability monitoring. Current time-series InSAR methods often apply generic temporal filtering or simple averaging, which can be biased by atmospheric noise, decorrelation, and the anisotropic scattering behavior of different land covers (e.g., forest vs. urban). This reduces the reliability of deformation signals, especially in complex terrain where early warning is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fusion framework which (1) selects a scene-type-specific scattering model and (2) weights multi-temporal observations based on data quality and atmospheric stability will yield more physically representative and statistically robust surface flux (deformation rate) estimates than standard persistent scatterer or small baseline approaches.
proposals.method: The framework first uses pre-existing land cover maps or SAR coherence/amplitude statistics to classify scene type (e.g., vegetated slope, rock outcrop, urban area). For each type, an appropriate anisotropic scattering model (e.g., Random Volume over Ground for forests, Point Scatterer for urban) is selected. Deformation time series are then estimated by solving a weighted least-squares inversion, where the weights for each SAR acquisition are dynamically assigned based on a composite quality score derived from per-pixel coherence, atmospheric phase screen (APS) estimates from ERA5/GACOS, and the temporal baseline. The temporal weighting function will prioritize observations from stable atmospheric conditions and high signal-to-noise ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel data fusion methodology that explicitly links surface physical properties to InSAR processing, moving beyond one-size-fits-all temporal models. It will deliver a more reliable deformation product for geohazard assessment, directly applicable to the researcher's focus on slope stability monitoring. The open-source algorithm will enable the community to improve deformation time-series robustness in diverse landscapes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent, automated selection of a physically appropriate scattering model per scene context *and* the dynamic, quality-aware temporal weighting of input SAR data, a fusion not commonly implemented in standard InSAR stacks or ground-based radar processing chains pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework for Probabilistic Landslide Deformation Monitoring via Fusion of Multi-Source SAR Data with Uncertain PSF Models
proposals.motivation: High-resolution, temporally dense surface deformation maps are critical for landslide early warning. Current multi-source SAR fusion methods (e.g., combining GB-InSAR and spaceborne InSAR) often assume a perfectly known imaging system, ignoring the spatially and temporally variable Point Spread Function (PSF) of sensors. This simplification introduces unquantified errors into downscaled displacement products, compromising the reliability of stability assessments for complex slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the PSF as a spatially and temporally variable probabilistic entity within a Bayesian data fusion framework will yield super-resolved deformation time series with more accurate and rigorously quantified uncertainty, leading to improved detection of precursory landslide movements.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model that jointly infers a super-resolution displacement field and the probabilistic state of the PSF. The prior for the displacement will incorporate spatiotemporal constraints from pre-2026 physical models (e.g., the MInTS approach). The PSF will be parameterized with a Gaussian Process prior to capture its variability. Inference will be performed using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, specifically a Gibbs sampler alternating between PSF parameter updates and high-resolution scene estimation, fusing multi-band observations from instruments like Sentinel-1 and ground-based radar.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, probabilistically rigorous fusion algorithm that quantifies uncertainty from both observational noise and instrumental ambiguity. It will deliver open-source software for generating high-resolution, uncertainty-aware deformation time series, directly enhancing the precision of slope stability evaluations for disaster risk reduction.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing fusion techniques that treat the PSF as fixed and known, this approach treats it as a primary object of inference with its own uncertainty, integrating this directly into a unified probabilistic downscaling model for geophysical parameter retrieval.

proposals.title: A PSF-Accurate Adversarial Framework for Sub-Pixel Cloud Phase Retrieval Using Multi-Sensor SAR Deformation Principles
proposals.motivation: Accurate cloud phase classification is critical for climate modeling and weather prediction, but satellite-based passive optical/IR retrievals are fundamentally limited by sensor spatial resolution and the point spread function (PSF), which convolves heterogeneous sub-pixel mixtures. Pre-2026 methods often treat pixels as homogeneous or use simple linear unmixing, neglecting the physical realism of sub-pixel cloud structure and the precise impact of the instrument's PSF, leading to significant biases in derived cloud properties.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly forward-modeling the generation of sub-pixel cloud phase heterogeneity and its convolution with the exact instrument PSF, a retrieval framework can invert sensor-level radiances to recover a more physically consistent and accurate high-resolution phase map than conventional methods that ignore this process.
proposals.method: We adapt principles from high-resolution SAR deformation monitoring (e.g., PSInSAR, ground-based radar tomography) to the cloud retrieval problem. A generative adversarial network (GAN), trained on high-resolution cloud-resolving model outputs and observations, will create plausible sub-scale distributions of liquid, ice, and mixed-phase elements. This scene is convolved with a pre-characterized instrument PSF and integrated to synthetic sensor radiances. An iterative optimization, using a differentiable forward model and a loss function combining radiance misfit and physical constraints (e.g., spatial smoothness, thermodynamic consistency), will adjust the generated scene to match actual satellite measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-informed retrieval framework that directly addresses the PSF convolution problem in cloud remote sensing. It will deliver improved accuracy in cloud phase and heterogeneity products for climate datasets. Furthermore, it demonstrates a transfer of advanced inversion methodologies from the geodetic radar community to atmospheric remote sensing.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit, end-to-end modeling of the PSF convolution process within the retrieval loop, moving beyond statistical unmixing. It uniquely combines adversarial scene generation from computer vision with rigorous physical forward modeling and optimization techniques inspired by advanced SAR interferometry.

proposals.title: A Scale-Aware Fusion Framework for Seamless Multi-Scale Deformation Composites from Ground-Based and Spaceborne SAR
proposals.motivation: Current multi-source SAR monitoring of landslides and slopes, combining ground-based (GB-SAR) and spaceborne (InSAR) data, often results in composite maps with sharp, artifact-prone boundaries at transition zones. These artifacts hinder quantitative analysis for regional hazard assessment and Earth system diagnostics. A method to create physically consistent, seamless deformation fields from inherently different spatial scales and resolutions is a critical unmet need in geohazard monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: A scale-aware blending algorithm, using scale-dependent weighting and spectral fusion, can integrate GB-SAR and InSAR deformation measurements into a seamless, artifact-free composite field that preserves the high-resolution detail of local surveys while maintaining the broad coverage and consistency of satellite data.
proposals.method: We will develop a fusion framework based on a multi-scale decomposition (e.g., using a Laplacian pyramid or wavelet transform) applied to the deformation velocity fields from each data source. Scale-dependent weighting functions will be designed using the inherent spatial resolution and measurement uncertainty of each dataset (GB-SAR and InSAR). The fusion will occur in the transformed domain, followed by reconstruction into a seamless composite. Techniques from image blending (e.g., gradient-domain fusion) will be adapted to minimize spectral discontinuities at data boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological pipeline for generating seamless, multi-scale deformation products, directly enhancing regional slope stability assessments. It will contribute a new, open-source tool for the InSAR/GB-SAR community, enabling more reliable integration of disparate SAR data sources for both disaster management and foundational geoscience research.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor fusion is common, the explicit, scale-aware blending for artifact removal in deformation composites is novel. This approach moves beyond simple mosaicking or statistical interpolation to a physically-guided spectral fusion tailored for geophysical signal preservation.

proposals.title: A Variable-Resolution Global Deformation Composite for Landslide Hazard Assessment by Integrating Multi-Source InSAR and Ground-Based Radar
proposals.motivation: Global landslide hazard assessment requires consistent, wide-area deformation baselines, but current InSAR-derived global products are limited by fixed, coarse resolution (e.g., 5 km), obscuring critical local-scale slope movements. Conversely, high-resolution data from ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and commercial satellites are spatially fragmented. A unified composite that intelligently merges these multi-scale datasets is needed to provide a seamless, context-rich deformation map for regional stability evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: A data fusion framework that generates a variable-resolution global deformation composite, integrating high-resolution data where available within a consistent 5-km baseline grid, will significantly improve the detection and preliminary characterization of active slope deformation zones at regional scales compared to using fixed-resolution products alone.
proposals.method: The method builds on Dr. Luo's prior multi-sensor fusion work. A core 5-km resolution global grid will be populated using open-access Sentinel-1 InSAR time-series data (e.g., using SBAS or PSI algorithms). Within each grid cell, higher-resolution data from available sources (e.g., COSMO-SkyMed, TerraSAR-X, or GB-SAR deformation fields) will be statistically aggregated (e.g., calculating mean velocity and variance) to refine the cell's value. A confidence layer will be generated based on data density and coherence. The composite will be validated against known landslide inventories (e.g., Global Landslide Catalog) and GNSS measurements in test regions like the Tibetan Plateau.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first openly available, variable-resolution global deformation composite specifically tuned for landslide precursor identification. It will provide a practical tool for prioritizing areas for detailed, local-scale monitoring (e.g., GB-SAR deployment) and enhance the integration of satellite and ground-based methods in operational disaster risk reduction frameworks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the fusion concept itself, but in its application to create a standardized, globally consistent yet locally enriched deformation product designed for landslide hazard screening, moving beyond site-specific case studies or uniform low-resolution mosaics.

proposals.title: A Generalized PSF Model for Multi-Constellation Data Fusion: Application to Landslide Deformation Monitoring
proposals.motivation: High-resolution, temporally dense surface deformation data is critical for landslide early warning and stability assessment. While multi-sensor fusion of optical (e.g., Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-2) and SAR data (e.g., Sentinel-1) offers a solution, the differing and often poorly characterized Point Spread Functions (PSFs) of these sensors introduce artifacts and inconsistencies during fusion, degrading the accuracy of derived displacement fields. A unified, sensor-agnostic PSF model is needed to enable physically consistent super-resolution and fusion, maximizing the utility of existing Earth observation constellations for geohazard monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: A generalized PSF model, cross-calibrated using a high-fidelity reference (e.g., EPIC's on-orbit data), can be applied to constrain the PSFs of other satellite sensors (Landsat, Sentinel-2), enabling a unified deconvolution framework that significantly improves the spatial resolution and consistency of fused multi-sensor datasets for landslide deformation analysis.
proposals.method: The method will first develop a parametric PSF model, initially validated with pre-2026 EPIC calibration data. This model will be used to deconvolve high-resolution reference imagery. We will then estimate the effective PSFs of target sensors (Sentinel-2 MSI, Landsat OLI) by solving an inverse problem that minimizes the difference between their observed images and the deconvolved reference, convolved with a candidate PSF. The resulting sensor-agnostic kernel library will be integrated into a super-resolution pipeline using a robust algorithm like the Van Cittert iteration or a regularized Lucy-Richardson deconvolution, applied to fused optical and InSAR-derived deformation time series.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source framework for PSF-consistent fusion of multi-constellation optical and SAR data, directly enhancing the spatial detail of deformation maps used in slope stability models. It will advance the field of multi-sensor landslide monitoring by moving beyond simple co-registration to physically-based image restoration, enabling more precise delineation of unstable areas and kinematic features.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a rigorously calibrated PSF from one sensor as a spatial constraint to infer the effective PSFs of other, operationally critical sensors, creating a unified deconvolution framework rather than applying isolated, sensor-specific corrections. This sensor-agnostic approach is explicitly designed for the heterogeneous constellation environment dominant in pre-2026 Earth observation.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Hierarchical Model for Multi-Sensor Deformation Fusion: Adapting Seismic Network Methods to GB-SAR and InSAR for Landslide Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Accurate landslide monitoring requires integrating deformation data from ground-based radar (GB-SAR) and spaceborne InSAR, which have different spatiotemporal resolutions, noise characteristics, and geometric sensitivities. Current fusion methods often lack a rigorous statistical framework to quantify uncertainties and reconcile discrepancies between these disparate sensors. A robust probabilistic fusion approach is needed to produce a unified, high-confidence deformation time series essential for early warning and stability evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian hierarchical model, analogous to those used in seismic network data fusion, can effectively merge GB-SAR and InSAR displacement time series to infer a most-probable deformation signal with quantified uncertainty, outperforming simple averaging or sequential integration methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework from multi-network seismology. The model will treat the true ground deformation as a latent variable, with GB-SAR and InSAR observations as data likelihoods parameterized by sensor-specific error models (e.g., atmospheric noise, geometric decorrelation). We will employ Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, specifically a Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) scheme via Stan or PyMC3, for inference. The model will be validated using historical data from a well-instrumented site like the Woda landslide, comparing fused results against GNSS ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous, uncertainty-aware framework for fusing ground-based and satellite radar data for slope stability assessment. It will deliver a reproducible open-source workflow for generating unified deformation products, enhancing the reliability of data interpretation for disaster risk reduction. The method will advance multi-sensor monitoring from qualitative combination to quantitative, probabilistic data assimilation.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods exist in InSAR, the direct adaptation of a hierarchical model structure from seismic network fusion to the specific problem of GB-SAR/InSAR integration is novel. This approach explicitly models the joint probability of all observations given a common truth, a conceptual shift from standard sequential correction or weighting techniques used pre-2026.

proposals.title: Adaptive Super-Resolution for Landslide Deformation Mapping via Multi-Temporal InSAR Fusion and a Learned Geomorphic Prior
proposals.motivation: High-resolution, wide-area deformation maps are critical for regional landslide hazard assessment but are constrained by the revisit time and resolution of individual SAR satellites. While multi-temporal InSAR (MT-InSAR) provides dense measurements, its spatial resolution is often insufficient for detailed slope-scale analysis. Fusing data from multiple sensors and revisits could enhance resolution, but robust algorithms to achieve this for deformation signals are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting multi-acquisition super-resolution techniques from medical MRI to multi-temporal SAR data stacks, and constraining the solution with a prior learned from high-resolution topography and optical data, we can generate deformation velocity maps at a spatial resolution finer than the native SAR sensor resolution, enabling more precise identification of unstable slopes.
proposals.method: The method treats multiple SAR acquisitions from Sentinel-1 (and/or ground-based radar) with slightly different viewing geometries as low-resolution, sub-pixel shifted inputs. A fusion algorithm based on robust multi-channel super-resolution will be implemented, incorporating Point Spread Function (PSF) deconvolution. The inversion will be regularized using a convolutional neural network (CNN) prior, trained on paired datasets of high-resolution LiDAR/optical-derived geomorphic features and co-registered, lower-resolution InSAR deformation patterns from known landslide areas.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel data fusion framework that pushes the practical resolution limit of wide-area InSAR monitoring for geohazards. It will provide a methodology to generate enhanced deformation products specifically tailored for landslide inventory mapping and precursory deformation detection at the slope scale, directly supporting disaster risk reduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-domain adaptation of a medical imaging super-resolution paradigm to geophysical deformation mapping and the integration of a data-driven geomorphic prior within the fusion process, moving beyond simple interpolation or statistical downscaling of InSAR data.

proposals.title: A Multi-Sensor Fusion Framework for Downscaling Regional InSAR Deformation Maps to Sub-Kilometer Resolution for Enhanced Landslide Susceptibility Assessment
proposals.motivation: Regional-scale landslide hazard assessment using spaceborne InSAR is often limited by the spatial resolution of deformation maps (typically >1 km), which obscures critical local variations in slope stability. High-resolution optical data (e.g., MODIS/VIIRS) contain rich surface texture and land cover information relevant to ground movement but are not directly used to refine deformation fields. Integrating these datasets can bridge the gap between regional monitoring and site-specific slope stability evaluation, a key challenge in pre-2026 disaster risk reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: That a compositing and Point Spread Function (PSF)-based convolution framework can effectively downscale regional InSAR mean velocity maps to sub-kilometer resolution by leveraging high-frequency spatial information from MODIS/VIIRS time-series composites, thereby revealing localized deformation patterns correlated with known landslide features.
proposals.method: The method will adapt a two-step fusion approach. First, annual MODIS/VIIRS surface reflectance composites (e.g., NDVI) will be generated to create high-resolution (250-500m) proxy stability indicators. Second, a PSF convolution model, informed by the sensor characteristics of the Sentinel-1 InSAR system, will be used to relate the high-resolution optical features to the lower-resolution InSAR deformation field. A regression-based downscaling algorithm (e.g., Area-to-Point Regression Kriging) will then be applied to disaggregate the regional InSAR map, constrained by the optical data and topographic derivatives from a pre-2026 digital elevation model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operational workflow for generating high-resolution regional deformation maps, directly enhancing the utility of InSAR for landslide inventory mapping and susceptibility modeling. It will provide a quantitative framework for fusing multi-sensor satellite data, moving beyond simple overlay analysis, to improve the spatial targeting of ground-based radar and GNSS monitoring campaigns in unstable mountainous regions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor fusion is common, the specific adaptation of PSF convolution and compositing techniques to downscale InSAR deformation products for landslide studies at a regional scale is novel. This approach moves beyond using optical data for visual interpretation, instead using it as a physical constraint to statistically enhance the resolution of geodetic measurements.

proposals.title: Cross-Dating Multi-Sensor Satellite Composites: A Dendrochronological Framework for Long-Term Land Surface Deformation Analysis
proposals.motivation: Long-term, consistent satellite records are critical for analyzing slow, climate-influenced geohazards like deep-seated landslides. However, constructing seamless multi-decadal time series from historical AVHRR and MODIS archives is challenged by sensor-specific biases, differing spatial/temporal resolutions, and data gaps. This inconsistency impedes the robust detection of long-term deformation trends and their climatic drivers, a core challenge in pre-2026 landslide hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical cross-dating principle from dendrochronology can be adapted to calibrate and splice AVHRR and MODIS composite data during their overlap period (2000-2020), creating a consistent, bias-corrected 'master chronology' of land surface observations suitable for long-term deformation studies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the cross-correlation and skeleton-plotting algorithms from dendrochronology. For key variables (e.g., land surface temperature, vegetation indices) during the AVHRR-MODIS overlap, we will extract statistically significant, overlapping spatio-temporal patterns ('fingerprints') to build a transfer function. This function will calibrate the longer AVHRR record to the MODIS standard. The method will be validated on known stable and deforming slopes, using InSAR and ground-based radar data from Yi Luo's previous work as ground truth for the spliced chronology's fidelity in capturing long-term surface changes.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel, statistically robust framework for generating seamless, multi-decadal satellite composite records. It will provide a new data foundation for analyzing the multi-year to decadal kinematics of large, climate-sensitive landslides. The method also offers a generalizable approach for harmonizing other multi-sensor Earth observation archives.
proposals.novelty: While sensor inter-calibration is established, applying the specific pattern-matching and master-chronology-building paradigm from dendrochronology to solve the temporal consistency problem in multi-sensor land surface composites is a novel interdisciplinary analogy. This moves beyond simple regression-based bias correction to a pattern-centric, statistical alignment strategy.

proposals.title: A Multi-Decadal, Near-Real-Time Satellite Composite Record for Climate Process Studies
proposals.motivation: Long-term climate data records (CDRs) from polar-orbiting satellites (e.g., AVHRR, MODIS) provide essential trend information but suffer from low temporal resolution, missing key diurnal processes. Conversely, geostationary satellites offer sub-hourly observations but lack long-term consistency and high-latitude coverage. A fused dataset bridging these scales is critical for understanding how short-term atmospheric dynamics and their statistics (e.g., diurnal temperature range, cloud lifecycle) have evolved over decades, directly addressing gaps in climate process attribution.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced cross-calibration and spatiotemporal fusion of polar and geostationary archives can produce a physically consistent, multi-decadal Earth observation record with sub-daily effective resolution, enabling the detection of statistically significant changes in the characteristics of high-frequency climate processes.
proposals.method: The methodology will integrate AVHRR (1981 onward) and MODIS (2000 onward) CDRs with contemporaneous geostationary data (e.g., GOES, Meteosat, Himawari). We will employ a multi-stage process: 1) Rigorous inter-sensor calibration using invariant targets and simultaneous nadir overpass (SNO) methods to harmonize radiances. 2) Application of a high-resolution spatiotemporal fusion model (e.g., an enhanced STARFM or FSDAF framework) adapted for multi-sensor, multi-decadal integration, leveraging the geostationary data's temporal profile to downscale polar records. 3) Generation of a continuous, gap-filled global composite product with a sub-2-hour refresh rate and associated uncertainty layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first operational, near-real-time climate data record that explicitly resolves diurnal cycles over a 40+ year period. It will provide a foundational dataset for studying long-term changes in process-scale phenomena, such as the intensification of the diurnal cycle of precipitation or shifts in cloud feedback timing, thereby linking climate trends to their mechanistic drivers.
proposals.novelty: While sensor inter-calibration and spatiotemporal fusion are established, creating a seamless, operationally sustained multi-decadal composite from the synergy of entire polar and geostationary fleets for climate process studies at sub-daily scales is an unprecedented integration challenge beyond current CDRs or reanalysis products.

proposals.title: A Temporally-Constrained Deconvolution Framework for Enhancing DSCOVR/EPIC Imagery Using Optical Flow from GEO/LEO Data for Land Surface and Cloud Dynamics Monitoring
proposals.motivation: The DSCOVR/EPIC instrument provides unique, continuous full-disk Earth observations but at a coarse spatial resolution (~10 km), limiting its ability to resolve critical short-term geophysical processes like landslide precursor deformation or rapid cloud evolution. High-resolution LEO satellites offer the necessary spatial detail but lack the high temporal cadence needed to track fast-evolving dynamics. There is a significant gap in methods to synergize these complementary data streams to create high-resolution, time-coherent Earth observations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating temporally dense optical flow vectors, derived from geostationary (e.g., Himawari-8, GOES-R) or historical high-resolution LEO data, as a physical motion constraint into the spatial deconvolution of EPIC data will enable the reconstruction of dynamically consistent, high-resolution (LEO-scale) time series of surface reflectance and cloud properties.
proposals.method: The method will adapt a variational data assimilation framework. First, optical flow (e.g., using Farnebäck or Lucas-Kanade methods) will be computed from high-cadence GEO data to define spatiotemporal motion fields. These fields will then be incorporated as a temporal regularization term within a cost function for deconvolving EPIC's single-view, low-resolution observations. The deconvolution kernel will be modeled based on pre-2026 EPIC point spread function characterizations. The optimization will be solved iteratively, constrained by the physics of advection from the optical flow, to estimate a sequence of high-resolution states that are both spatially sharp and temporally coherent.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source processing chain that generates synthetic LEO-resolution image sequences from EPIC data, revealing sub-daily surface and atmospheric dynamics. It will provide a new tool for monitoring transient ground deformation signals and cloud processes. The methodology will advance multi-sensor fusion by demonstrating how temporal information from one sensor can physically regularize the spatial enhancement of another.
proposals.novelty: While image deconvolution and super-resolution for satellite data exist, the explicit integration of independently derived optical flow as a hard physical constraint to guide the temporal evolution within the deconvolution inverse problem is a novel approach. This moves beyond simple fusion or interpolation, enforcing dynamic consistency.

ideas.title: Dynamic, High-Resolution Monitoring of Fast-Changing Environmental Indices via Geostationary-Guided Sentinel-2 Downscaling
ideas.description: This research fuses the extension of Sentinel-2 downscaling methods to new band combinations with the integration of high-temporal-resolution geostationary data. The core innovation is to use the dense temporal signal from geostationary satellites (e.g., from Himawari-8/9, GOES-R, or MSG) not just to reduce composite lag, but as a dynamic prior to guide the spatial downscaling of multiple Sentinel-2 indices. This enables the reconstruction of high-resolution index time series at the native refresh rate of the geostationary data, even for indices that rely on Sentinel-2's lower-resolution bands. The result is a novel framework for monitoring rapidly evolving phenomena—such as flood propagation, fire fronts, or convective cloud development—with both the spatial detail of Sentinel-2 and the near-real-time temporal perspective of geostationary observation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Spectral-Guided Fusion for High-Refresh SWIR Monitoring
ideas.description: This research fuses high-temporal-resolution geostationary data with high-spatial-resolution multispectral data to create a novel downscaling framework for SWIR bands. Instead of statically using green or red-edge bands as guides, the method analyzes the high-refresh-rate geostationary data stream to dynamically select the guiding band from the high-resolution sensor that best correlates with the SWIR signal under the current illumination and atmospheric state. This adaptive, condition-aware approach minimizes the temporal lag between the guide and target SWIR data to under two hours, producing more accurate and physically consistent high-resolution SWIR composites for monitoring rapidly evolving phenomena like soil moisture or vegetation stress.

ideas.title: Adaptive Pixel Super-Resolution via Multi-Sensor Data Fusion and Learned Priors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a super-resolution technique from medical MRI to the problem of generating high-resolution regional maps from moderate-resolution sensors like MODIS or VIIRS. In MRI, high-resolution images are reconstructed from multiple low-resolution, offset scans using a fusion algorithm constrained by a structural prior. The adapted solution applies this to satellite data by treating multiple overpasses as the offset low-resolution inputs. The compositing and PSF deconvolution process is then enhanced with a data-driven prior (learned from available high-res data) or a model-based prior (e.g., expected spatial patterns per land cover class). This constrained reconstruction allows for the reliable downscaling of MODIS/VIIRS data to sub-kilometer resolutions for detailed regional studies, overcoming the inherent limitations of the sensor's native pixel size.

ideas.title: Automated anisotropic downscaling for dynamic global water flux monitoring
ideas.description: This research develops an operational pipeline that fuses high-resolution (10-m) global MNDWI time series generation with automated anisotropic model selection. The system first downscales coarse-resolution water data to create consistent global 10-m water masks. It then analyzes the spatiotemporal patterns within these high-resolution composites to automatically identify scene-specific dynamics—such as tidal flows, riverine flooding, or coastal erosion—and selects the appropriate directional model to calculate water flux. This enables the simultaneous delivery of both high-resolution water extent and dynamically modeled flux vectors globally, providing unprecedented insight into the movement and change of surface water at a local scale.

ideas.title: Temporally-Guided, Physics-Informed Deconvolution of EPIC Data to LEO-Resolved Cloud and Surface Dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a novel inverse method that fuses the spatial deconvolution of DSCOVR's EPIC observations with temporal guidance from optical flow derived from geostationary or previous high-resolution LEO data. The optical flow provides a physical constraint on the motion and evolution of cloud and surface features, which regularizes the ill-posed deconvolution problem, making it tractable to estimate high-resolution states. This synergy enables the production of time-coherent, dynamically consistent sequences of cloud properties and surface reflectance at LEO-like spatial scales from the single-view, low-frequency EPIC data, revealing short-term processes not directly observable by EPIC alone.

ideas.title: Single-instrument climatology is sufficient

ideas.title: A Multi-Decadal, Near-Real-Time Satellite Composite Record for Climate Process Studies
ideas.description: This research synthesizes long-term polar-orbiter archives (AVHRR, MODIS) with high-temporal-resolution geostationary data to generate a novel, consistent multi-decadal climate data record with an effective refresh rate of less than two hours. The synergy creates a dataset that uniquely bridges climate-scale trends with sub-daily process-scale dynamics, such as tracking the evolution of diurnal temperature cycles or cloud feedbacks over decades. This allows for unprecedented analysis of how short-term atmospheric processes and their statistics have changed over the climate record. The methodology must develop advanced cross-calibration and fusion techniques to ensure radiometric consistency across the vastly different sensor characteristics and orbital geometries involved.

ideas.title: A Generalized, Sensor-Agnostic PSF Model for Multi-Constellation Data Fusion and Super-Resolution
ideas.description: This research develops a generalized point spread function (PSF) modeling framework, initially validated with EPIC's on-orbit calibration data, that is explicitly designed for application across multiple satellite constellations like Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-2, and others with mixed resolutions. The core innovation is to use the rigorously calibrated PSF from one sensor to inform and constrain the PSF models of other sensors, creating a unified, sensor-agnostic convolution kernel. This enables physically consistent downscaling, data fusion, and super-resolution techniques across different platforms, significantly improving the interoperability and combined spatial resolution of multi-sensor Earth observation datasets beyond what is possible with isolated, sensor-specific PSF corrections.

ideas.title: Bayesian Fusion of Spatiotemporally Variable PSF Models for Probabilistic High-Resolution Earth Observation
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the modeling of a spatially and temporally variable Point Spread Function (PSF) for instruments like those on EPIC with Bayesian data fusion methods for downscaling. The core innovation is to treat the PSF not as a fixed known parameter but as a probabilistic entity with its own uncertainty, which is then integrated into a hierarchical Bayesian model for multi-band data fusion. This allows for the simultaneous inference of the super-resolution scene and the most probable PSF state given the observed, blurred data, inherently correcting for instrument changes over time. The output is a probabilistically downscaled product where the uncertainty estimates rigorously incorporate both spectral fusion errors and the fundamental ambiguity introduced by an imperfectly known imaging system.

ideas.title: Gap-Free Global 10-m Water Dynamics through Multi-Sensor Fusion and Physically-Guided Imputation
ideas.description: This research designs an operational system to generate a globally complete, daily 10-m MNDWI-equivalent time series by fusing multi-sensor satellite data. It overcomes the fatal flaw of cloud-induced gaps by integrating Sentinel-2 (optical), Sentinel-1 (SAR), and Landsat data within a physics-informed deep learning architecture that imputes missing high-resolution values using temporal context and all-weather SAR backscatter. The output is a seamless, gap-free water mask and turbidity proxy product with quantified uncertainty, enabling reliable high-resolution monitoring of water extent, floods, and droughts even in perpetually cloudy regions.

ideas.title: Forward Modeling of Sub-Pixel Cloud Phase Heterogeneity for PSF-Accurate Retrieval
ideas.description: This research develops a retrieval framework that explicitly models sub-pixel cloud phase heterogeneity before PSF convolution occurs. A high-resolution scene generator creates plausible distributions of liquid, ice, and mixed-phase cloud elements based on physical and dynamical constraints. This high-resolution scene is then forward-convolved with the exact instrument point spread function and spatial integration to produce synthetic sensor-level observations. The retrieval algorithm iteratively adjusts the high-resolution phase map to minimize the difference between these synthetic observations and the actual measurements, thereby solving for the most physically consistent sub-pixel structure that explains the convolved signal.

ideas.title: Harmonizing Spectral Resolution via Upscaling: A Geophysical Data Fusion Approach
ideas.description: This idea is expanded by analogy to data fusion in geophysics, specifically seismic imaging. In that field, disparate wave datasets are often harmonized to a consistent, coarser resolution prior to joint analysis to ensure model stability and consistency. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate upscaling all Sentinel-2 bands to a uniform 20m resolution. The key adaptation is employing a sophisticated upscaling method informed by the sensor's PSF, rather than naive interpolation, to properly model the information loss. The core hypothesis is that this intentional resolution harmonization will yield a more internally consistent multi-spectral dataset, where the relationships between bands are physically coherent, ultimately leading to better classification performance by reducing artifacts and noise introduced by native resolution mismatches.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Data Fusion for Multi-Sensor Radiometric Merging
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from geophysics to the original computational photography concept. In seismology, data from disparate sensor networks are fused using Bayesian hierarchical models to estimate a common ground truth signal, accounting for each instrument's unique characteristics. Adapted to remote sensing or astronomy, this approach would use a similar probabilistic framework to merge radiances from sensors with different calibrations, spectral responses, and noise floors. The model would infer the most probable high-dynamic-range radiance map, along with uncertainty estimates, providing a statistically robust alternative to standard HDR merging techniques for scientific multi-sensor data fusion.

ideas.title: Seamless Multi-Scale Global Composite via Scale-Aware Data Fusion
ideas.description: This system generates a globally seamless environmental composite by fusing multi-source data at their native resolutions through a scale-aware blending algorithm. Instead of creating a mosaic with hard boundaries, the framework uses techniques like scale-dependent weighting and spectral fusion to integrate high-resolution land observations with coarser oceanic and atmospheric data smoothly. The output is a physically consistent composite field suitable for both visual analysis and quantitative applications, such as climate modeling or Earth system diagnostics, without introducing artifacts at data transition zones.

ideas.title: Applying Dendrochronological Cross-Dating to Multi-Sensor Satellite Composites
ideas.description: This research adapts the cross-dating methodology from dendrochronology to solve the problem of scaling up to consistent multi-decadal climate data records from historical AVHRR and MODIS archives. In dendrochronology, scientists build continuous, millennia-long tree-ring chronologies by statistically matching overlapping patterns from samples of different ages to create a master timeline. Analogously, we will apply this core principle to satellite data by using the period of simultaneous AVHRR and MODIS operations (the 'overlap zone') as a reference. By developing statistical pattern-matching algorithms for key atmospheric and surface variables in the hourly composites during this overlap, we can calibrate the longer AVHRR record to the MODIS standard. This process creates a seamlessly spliced 'master chronology' of hourly composites extending back to the early AVHRR period, ensuring long-term consistency, correcting for sensor-specific biases, and providing a robust framework for gap-filling across the entire multi-sensor archive.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Quality-Weighted Anisotropic Model Selection for Surface Flux Estimation
ideas.description: This research develops an automated framework that fuses scene-based anisotropic model selection with quality-aware temporal data weighting. The system first identifies the composite scene type (e.g., forest, urban, water) to select an appropriate bidirectional reflectance distribution function (BRDF) or directional emissivity model. It then populates this model not just with the nearest-time observations, but with a weighted ensemble of observations from a relevant temporal window, where weights are determined by per-observation quality scores and atmospheric stability metrics. This results in a final flux calculation that is both physically representative of the surface type and statistically robust against transient atmospheric noise and data gaps.

ideas.title: Adapting Super-Resolution Microscopy Techniques for Multi-Sensor Satellite Data Fusion
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from super-resolution microscopy to the problem of downscaling satellite data. In microscopy, techniques like STORM/PALM fuse many low-resolution, stochastic frames to create a high-resolution image that surpasses the optical diffraction limit. Similarly, for satellite constellations like Landsat 8/9 or combined with Sentinel-2, we can treat multiple, temporally close but spatially offset satellite passes over the same target as a set of 'snapshots.' Each snapshot has a fixed, medium resolution, but the sub-pixel offsets between passes contain complementary information. By adapting the localization and fusion algorithms from super-resolution microscopy, we can develop a novel downscaling method that computationally synthesizes a higher-resolution image from this set of mixed-resolution, multi-temporal observations, effectively scaling the concept to leverage the growing volume of data from modern satellite constellations.

ideas.title: Physics-Guided, Learning-Based Fusion for Physically Consistent Sub-Kilometer Surface Reflectance Mapping
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple downscaling by developing a hybrid physical-statistical system. It uses a convolutional neural network architecture trained to fuse temporally dense MODIS/VIIRS data with spatially detailed but less frequent Sentinel-2/Landsat imagery, learning the mapping between low-resolution composites and actual high-resolution scenes. The model is constrained by physical principles, such as spectral band relationships and temporal phenology models, to prevent hallucination of features. The output is a high-resolution (e.g., 20-100m) time series that maintains the temporal fidelity of the coarse input while embedding statistically and physically realistic spatial detail learned from coincident high-resolution observations.

ideas.title: Bayesian Fusion for Downscaling via Seismic Tomography-Inspired Uncertainty Integration
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Bayesian hierarchical data fusion solution from geophysical seismic tomography to the problem of probabilistic downscaling in remote sensing. In seismic tomography, disparate data types (seismic, gravity, etc.) with different resolutions and uncertainties are integrated within a Bayesian framework using error covariance matrices to weight each dataset's contribution, producing a refined subsurface model with uncertainty estimates. The adapted research idea applies this analogous solution by treating each remote sensing band or sensor input as an independent data source with a characterized uncertainty. A Bayesian fusion model is constructed with a prior distribution representing expected high-resolution structure and likelihood functions for each band that incorporate their specific spatial resolution and radiometric uncertainty. The joint posterior distribution yields a probabilistically downscaled, high-resolution product where the uncertainty from each input band is rigorously propagated, providing a robust, uncertainty-aware data product for subsequent analysis.

ideas.title: Physics-based inter-sensor harmonization for a radiometrically consistent AVHRR-MODIS climate data record
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first creates a unified, sensor-agnostic radiance record by applying a rigorous physics-based harmonization. It uses a radiative transfer model and a global network of invariant calibration sites to derive spectral and geometric adjustment kernels that normalize AVHRR data to MODIS-like characteristics. This corrected, top-of-atmosphere radiance time series then serves as the input to the hourly compositing methodology, ensuring the final multi-decadal product is suitable for robust trend analysis. The system outputs uncertainty estimates for the harmonization step alongside the final composites, quantifying confidence in the long-term record.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Deep Learning for Spatiotemporal Super-Resolution of SWIR Data
ideas.description: This research fuses a convolutional neural network (CNN) for single-image super-resolution of Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR) data with a numerical weather prediction (NWP) model-based data assimilation system. The CNN is not trained solely on paired low/high-resolution image data but is integrated into an assimilation cycle where the NWP model provides prior estimates of atmospheric state and surface conditions. This allows the network to learn a super-resolution mapping that is dynamically regularized by physical constraints, enabling it to intelligently reconstruct high-resolution SWIR scenes in data gaps by assimilating available low-resolution observations with the model forecast. The result is a continuous, gap-free, high-resolution SWIR product that is both visually sharp and physically consistent with the governing meteorological and radiative processes.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Global Data Composites
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a uniform, fixed-resolution grid for global composites by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) solves the problem of simulating complex phenomena efficiently by using fine computational grids only where needed (e.g., near shocks or boundaries) and coarse grids elsewhere. Adapting this solution, the research proposes an adaptive, variable-resolution compositing system for global geospatial data. The system would dynamically assess the spatial density and native resolution of all input data streams (e.g., satellite imagery, in-situ sensors) and generate a composite using a hierarchical, multi-scale tiling scheme. This results in a final product where land areas with abundant high-resolution data are rendered at fine detail, while oceanic and other data-sparse regions use a coarser, more efficient representation, optimizing both information content and computational/storage resources.

ideas.title: Adaptive Temporal Filtering for Video Downscaling Using Optical Flow Priors
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from seismic data processing. In that field, plane-wave destruction filters use estimated local slopes (dip fields) to guide the interpolation and denoising of spatially aliased data along coherent temporal events. The adapted solution applies this principle to video downscaling. Dense optical flow between consecutive high-resolution frames is computed to create a motion vector field, analogous to a seismic dip field. This temporal guide is then used to steer a content-adaptive downscaling kernel. The kernel samples and weights pixels from the current and previous high-resolution frames not just spatially, but along the predicted motion trajectories, effectively using the high-resolution past as a prior to enhance the detail and temporal consistency of the downscaled video output.

ideas.title: Multi-Spectral HDR Fusion for High-Fidelity SWIR Sharpening
ideas.description: This research synthesizes computational HDR merging with multi-band guided downscaling to enhance SWIR imagery. Instead of using a single high-resolution band (like green) to guide SWIR downscaling, the method employs an HDR-inspired algorithm to merge radiance information from multiple candidate guide bands (e.g., red-edge, red, NIR). The core innovation is treating the differently calibrated sensor data for each guide band as 'exposures' of the same scene, using HDR techniques to combine them into a superior, noise-reduced, high-resolution guide image that optimally preserves spectral relationships with the target SWIR band. This fused guide then drives the final SWIR downscaling, yielding a product with superior spatial detail and radiometric fidelity for applications like mineral mapping or vegetation stress detection.

ideas.title: Calibrating the EPIC PSF via a Star Catalog Reference Frame
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a static point spread function (PSF) for the EPIC instrument by proposing a calibration method adapted from astronomical spectroscopy. In that field, the variable line spread function of spectrographs is mapped and corrected using observations of standard stars within a fixed celestial reference frame. For EPIC, we adapt this solution by implementing an internal, invariant reference source—such as a calibration fiber or pinhole array—to frequently sample the PSF at key positions across the sensor. This data will be used to build a dynamic, high-fidelity model of the PSF's spatial and temporal variability due to instrument changes, enabling precise deconvolution and significantly improving the accuracy of photometric and morphological measurements in EPIC data.

ideas.title: Multi-Sensor Dynamic Fusion for Optimized Spatiotemporal Satellite Composites
ideas.description: This system overcomes the spatial-resolution penalty of high-refresh geostationary data by implementing an intelligent, adaptive fusion engine. It ingests real-time streams from both geostationary constellations (e.g., GOES, Himawari) and polar-orbiting satellites (e.g., Sentinel, Landsat, MODIS), leveraging their complementary strengths. A core machine learning model, trained to understand scene-specific relationships between sensor characteristics, performs pixel-level statistical correction and blending. The output is a continuous, high-fidelity composite that maintains sub-two-hour latency while preserving the spatial detail critical for accurate analysis.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Stochastic-Deterministic PSF Model for EPIC
ideas.description: This approach develops a comprehensive PSF model for missions like EPIC that accounts for both predictable and unpredictable variability. It uses a two-tiered structure: a static, instrument-model-based component for field-dependent and scheduled thermal effects, and a dynamic, data-constrained component for stochastic temporal variations. The system continuously updates the probabilistic description of the PSF using the ensemble of stars in each exposure, directly marginalizing over this uncertainty during the extraction of photometry and centroids. This results in more robust error estimates and mitigates biases caused by assuming an imperfectly known PSF.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Sensor Data Fusion with Kalman Filtering for Real-Time Satellite Composites
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the multi-sensor data fusion solution from autonomous vehicle navigation to the problem of reducing temporal lag in satellite composites. In autonomous systems, a Kalman filter fuses asynchronous data from LiDAR, radar, and cameras to create a single, optimal, real-time estimate of the vehicle's surroundings. Analogously, we propose to treat geostationary (high refresh rate) and polar-orbiting (high spatial detail) satellite data as inputs to a dynamic data assimilation system. By implementing an Ensemble Kalman Filter or similar Bayesian technique, the system would continuously update an environmental state estimate. The geostationary data provides frequent updates to predict changes, while the less frequent polar data provides precise 'ground truth' corrections. This fusion creates a seamless, continuously evolving composite with an effective refresh rate far superior to simple compositing, potentially reducing the perceived temporal lag to minutes while preserving spatial detail.

ideas.title: Regionally-Adaptive Data Assimilation for Downscaling and Gap-Filling in Diverse Land-Water Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a unified downscaling and gap-filling framework that integrates numerical weather prediction (NWP) models via data assimilation, specifically designed and tested across highly diverse geographical regions. The core innovation is to use the challenge of different landscapes and water types—each with distinct radiative, thermal, and dynamic properties—to train and validate an adaptive assimilation system. The method will automatically adjust background error covariances, observation operators, and balance constraints within the assimilation loop based on the detected surface regime, ensuring optimal reconstruction of high-resolution fields whether over homogenous oceans or heterogeneous urban coasts. This results in a generically applicable tool that provides physically consistent, gap-filled, high-resolution environmental composites without requiring region-specific parameter tuning.

ideas.title: Separate Convolution Kernels for Cloud Phases Using Spectral Unmixing Principles
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a single Point Spread Function (PSF) convolution kernel is appropriate for mixed-phase cloud pixels in remote sensing data. Drawing an analogy from hyperspectral imaging's solution to the mixed-pixel problem via spectral unmixing, we propose adapting the linear mixing model concept to the spatial domain. The core idea is to characterize separate spatial response functions (PSFs) for pure liquid and pure ice cloud phases, acknowledging their different microphysical structures and scattering properties. For a mixed-phase pixel, the observed spatial signal post-convolution would be modeled as the sum of the liquid phase fraction convolved with the 'liquid PSF' and the ice phase fraction convolved with the 'ice PSF'. This approach requires prior estimation of sub-pixel phase fractions (e.g., from spectral signatures or ancillary data) and the development of phase-specific kernel libraries. The investigation would focus on whether this separate processing during convolution reduces blurring artifacts, improves the spatial fidelity of retrieved phase maps, and mitigates errors in downstream products like cloud optical thickness or particle size for mixed-phase clouds.

ideas.title: Graph-Optimized Variable-Resolution Compositing for Global Spatiotemporal Consistency
ideas.description: This research fuses variable-resolution compositing with graph-based optimization to develop a novel framework for generating global environmental data products. The core idea is to model available satellite observations and their spatiotemporal relationships as a graph, where nodes represent observations with inherent resolutions and edges represent consistency constraints. An optimization algorithm then selects the optimal subset of observations to construct a composite that maximizes global consistency and information content, strategically using higher-resolution data (e.g., over land) where available while seamlessly blending it with coarser data to fill gaps without introducing artifacts. This results in a physically coherent, gap-free global composite where the effective resolution varies intelligently based on source data density and quality, governed by the overarching optimization objective.

ideas.title: Scene-Adaptive PSF Modeling for Anisotropic Flux Retrieval in Composite Scenes
ideas.description: This research develops an automated framework that integrates scene classification with PSF characterization to improve flux accuracy in composite scenes. The system first identifies scene elements (e.g., vegetation canopies, urban structures, water bodies) and their anisotropic properties, then selects the most appropriate directional reflectance model. Crucially, it concurrently selects or constructs a scene-aware PSF model, refined with on-orbit calibration data, that accounts for the instrument's directional sensitivity and the scene's spatial heterogeneity. This joint optimization ensures the deconvolution process physically aligns with the anisotropic scattering properties of the identified materials, yielding more accurate top-of-atmosphere and surface-level flux retrievals than possible by treating model selection and PSF correction as separate problems.

ideas.title: Causal Factor Isolation for Pan-sharpening Robustness via Synthetic Landscape Generation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive regional testing to establish causal links between landscape characteristics and pan-sharpening performance. A simulation engine creates controlled, hybrid satellite scenes where challenging factors like mixed water-land boundaries, specific building densities, or vegetation types are systematically varied in isolation and combination. The pan-sharpening method is then evaluated against this synthetic benchmark to quantify its sensitivity to each factor. This approach generates a diagnostic 'failure map' for the algorithm, providing direct, actionable insights for physics-aware model redesign rather than just a report of regional performance disparities.

ideas.title: A Unified Deep Learning Framework for Cross-Sensor, Cross-Resolution Downscaling Beyond Pan-Sharpening
ideas.description: This research proposes developing a unified deep learning framework for downscaling that is explicitly designed to work across multiple satellite constellations (e.g., Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-2, PlanetScope) with varying band configurations and resolution mixtures. Instead of applying traditional pan-sharpening or single-sensor super-resolution, the model will learn a cross-sensor mapping to generate high-resolution, multi-spectral imagery by intelligently fusing information from the different resolution layers available across these diverse systems. This approach challenges pan-sharpening as a one-size-fits-all solution by creating an adaptive method that can leverage the unique characteristics of each constellation to achieve superior downscaling performance, enabling consistent, high-quality data generation from a heterogeneous fleet of Earth observation assets.

ideas.title: Physics-Guided, Spectrally-Anchored Fusion for Distortion-Free Downscaling of Green and SWIR Bands
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the critical flaw of spectral distortion by using a dual-path, physics-informed fusion approach. For the green band, it performs conventional pan-sharpening guided by the high-resolution panchromatic data. For the SWIR band, it fuses the panchromatic data with a spectrally-anchoring band (e.g., a lower-resolution thermal band) within a deep network architecture that is explicitly regularized by physical relationships between SWIR reflectance and material properties. The model is trained on a dataset generated from radiative transfer simulations to learn the correct mapping without introducing spurious correlations, ensuring the downscaled SWIR data remains physically meaningful for applications like vegetation stress and mineral mapping.

ideas.title: Joint Radiance Field and Sensor Calibration Estimation via Probabilistic Multi-Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This research develops a unified computational framework that simultaneously estimates a high-dynamic-range scene radiance map and the precise, per-acquisition calibration parameters of multiple heterogeneous sensors. Instead of assuming known calibrations, the system treats sensor radiometric models—including gain, offset, and non-linearity—as variables to be optimized, using overlapping observations and physical constraints as evidence. This approach robustly merges data from sensors with unknown, differing, or drifting calibrations, producing physically accurate radiance estimates essential for scientific remote sensing and planetary exploration.
